proposals.title: Bootstrapping High-Precision Clinical Descriptor Extraction via Unsupervised LLM Pre-Annotation and Multi-Agent Refinement
proposals.motivation: Extracting structured clinical descriptors from unstructured medical notes is crucial for downstream tasks like patient phenotyping and outcome prediction, but creating large-scale, high-quality labeled datasets is prohibitively expensive. While unsupervised or weakly-supervised methods using LLMs offer a scalable alternative, their performance often plateaus below the reliability required for clinical decision support. This work addresses the need for a cost-effective pipeline that bridges the scalability of unsupervised methods with the precision of supervised models.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that an unsupervised multi-agent LLM system can generate sufficiently reliable 'silver-standard' annotations from raw clinical text to effectively bootstrap a smaller, supervised discriminative model (e.g., a fine-tuned transformer), which will then surpass the performance of the unsupervised system alone.
proposals.method: We will first design an unsupervised multi-agent system, inspired by recent 'Agent Paradox' explorations, where specialized LLM agents (e.g., for entity identification, relation extraction, normalization) collaborate to annotate a large corpus of unlabeled bladder cancer notes. This system's outputs, along with confidence scores, will form a silver-standard dataset. We will then train a supervised model (e.g., a ClinicalBERT or BioBERT variant) on this bootstrapped data, using a small subset of expert-labeled notes for validation and final calibration. The framework will be evaluated in-the-wild for robustness.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel, practical framework for building high-performance clinical NLP systems with reduced annotation burden. It will provide empirical evidence on the transferability of knowledge from complex, unsupervised multi-agent systems to more efficient supervised models, and release a benchmark dataset of LLM-generated annotations for bladder cancer descriptors.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in inverting the typical role of unsupervised methods from an end-point to a bootstrapping mechanism for supervision, and in explicitly leveraging the structured reasoning of a multi-agent LLM system as a scalable pre-annotation tool, rather than using simpler prompting or embedding-based methods.

proposals.title: Sentence-Level Clinical Concept Grading: A Fine-Grained Evaluation Framework for LLM-Generated Definitions
proposals.motivation: Large language models (LLMs) are increasingly used in clinical education and information extraction, but existing evaluation frameworks often operate at the document or paragraph level, lacking granularity. For high-stakes domains like medicine, assessing the correctness of a single-sentence definition or a fill-in-the-blank response is critical for reliable automated tutoring and assessment. Pre-2026 work, including Mihalcea's own on variability in clinical extraction, highlights the need for robust, fine-grained evaluation methods for AI systems 'in the wild'.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a specialized, multi-faceted evaluation system combining descriptor-based scoring and LLM-as-a-judge paradigms can achieve higher accuracy and reliability in grading single-sentence clinical responses than existing holistic or keyword-matching methods.
proposals.method: We will adapt Mihalcea's 'Evaluation Framework for AI Systems' to the sentence level. The method involves: 1) Creating a labeled dataset of correct/incorrect clinical definitions and fill-in responses, annotated by experts with error descriptors (e.g., 'omission', 'hallucination', 'imprecision'). 2) Developing a multi-agent evaluation system where one LLM agent (e.g., GPT-4) generates candidate sentences, and a separate 'grader' agent, guided by the descriptor taxonomy and reference knowledge, scores them. 3) Employing contrastive and few-shot learning techniques to fine-tune a smaller, specialized model (e.g., ClinicalBERT) for this grading task, comparing its performance to the LLM-based agent system.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel, publicly available benchmark for sentence-level clinical concept grading. It will provide empirical evidence on the trade-offs between complex multi-agent evaluation systems and fine-tuned specialized models for fine-grained assessment. The resulting framework will be a tool for developing more reliable AI tutors and clinical note review systems.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the granular application of a multi-agent evaluation framework to the constrained but critical problem of single-sentence grading, moving beyond document-level analysis. It uniquely combines structured error descriptors with flexible LLM-based judgment to model the nuanced decision-making of a human grader.

proposals.title: DUAL: Generating Calibrated Exemplar Answers from Rubrics for Automated Performance Assessment
proposals.motivation: Automated scoring of complex, open-ended responses in education and clinical assessments relies on high-quality, labeled training data, which is expensive and time-consuming to produce. Existing methods (pre-2026) primarily focus on learning scoring models from rubrics and human-graded examples, but the inverse process—generating diverse, rubric-aligned answer exemplars—remains underexplored. This limits the scalability and robustness of assessment systems, especially for new domains or nuanced performance criteria where labeled data is scarce.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a dual-task framework, leveraging instruction-tuned large language models (LLMs), can invert the scoring paradigm to generate high-fidelity, multi-level exemplar answers directly from descriptive rubrics, and that these synthetic exemplars can effectively augment training data to improve automated scoring model performance.
proposals.method: We propose a multi-agent, controlled generation system. A primary LLM (e.g., GPT-4 or Llama 2), conditioned on a structured rubric with performance level descriptors, will generate candidate answers. A secondary 'critic' agent, using a smaller model fine-tuned on human-scored data, will evaluate alignment and provide iterative feedback. We will employ constrained decoding and reinforcement learning from human feedback (RLHF) techniques to ensure outputs span the rubric's quality spectrum. The method will be validated on clinical note abstraction tasks (extending Mihalcea's prior work) and Japanese language proficiency essay scoring, assessing the utility of synthetic data for training downstream scoring models.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel, generalizable framework for data augmentation in performance assessment, reducing dependency on large human-labeled datasets. It will provide new insights into the interpretability of rubrics as generative constraints for LLMs and deliver a publicly available system for creating calibrated assessment exemplars.
proposals.novelty: While prior work uses rubrics to *score* answers, our approach inverts this to *generate* answers, creating a closed-loop system for assessment. The multi-agent, iterative refinement process for ensuring rubric fidelity moves beyond simple prompt engineering, addressing the 'Agent Paradox' of replicating nuanced human judgment in generative tasks.

proposals.title: Beyond Well-Formedness: Evaluating Semantic Textual Similarity on Noisy, Informal, and Clinical Language
proposals.motivation: Semantic Textual Similarity (STS) benchmarks predominantly use well-formed text, creating a significant gap in evaluating real-world applications where language is informal, ungrammatical, or noisy (e.g., social media, clinical notes, user-generated content). This limitation is critical for deploying robust NLP systems in clinical and 'in-the-wild' settings, as highlighted by Mihalcea's prior work on clinical information extraction and evaluation frameworks for AI systems in the wild. The performance of modern LLMs on such noisy data remains poorly understood.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that current STS models and benchmarks significantly overestimate real-world performance, and that LLM-based approaches, particularly multi-agent or instruction-tuned systems, can be more robust to linguistic noise and informality than traditional supervised models when provided with appropriate task descriptors.
proposals.method: We will construct a novel, multi-domain benchmark for 'Noisy STS' by aggregating and manually labeling pairs from existing clinical note datasets (e.g., MIMIC-III), social media corpora, and synthetically noisified versions of standard STS data (e.g., STS-B). We will evaluate a spectrum of models: (1) traditional supervised STS models (e.g., SBERT), (2) large language models (LLMs) like GPT-4 and Llama 2 under zero-shot and few-shot prompting with tailored descriptors, and (3) a novel multi-agent system where specialized agents (e.g., for normalization, coreference, paraphrasing) collaboratively analyze text pairs before a final similarity judgment, inspired by the 'Agent Paradox' framework. Performance will be analyzed relative to noise type and degree.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first comprehensive evaluation framework for STS on noisy, real-world text, directly contributing to more reliable NLP deployment in clinical and social computing domains. It will yield a public benchmark dataset and new insights into the failure modes of LLMs and the potential of multi-agent architectures for handling linguistic complexity and variability.
proposals.novelty: While prior work has focused on information extraction from noisy text, systematically challenging the fundamental 'well-formed text' assumption of core NLP tasks like STS is novel. Furthermore, exploring multi-agent systems as a method to explicitly manage different types of noise and informality for similarity assessment is an unexplored approach.

proposals.title: Beyond Human Judgments: A Multi-Agent, Task-Driven Framework for Semantic Textual Similarity Evaluation
proposals.motivation: Semantic Textual Similarity (STS) benchmarks rely heavily on costly and potentially inconsistent human similarity judgments, limiting scalability and real-world applicability. This is particularly problematic in clinical and other high-stakes domains where evaluation 'in the wild' is crucial. A new paradigm is needed to create robust, task-grounded STS metrics that do not depend on direct human annotations for training or validation.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a multi-agent system, where agents with distinct roles (e.g., a clinical summarizer, a query generator, a consistency checker) collaborate on a downstream task, can generate intrinsic performance signals that correlate with, and can serve as a proxy for, human similarity judgments.
proposals.method: We will design a multi-agent framework where agents, built upon specialized LLMs (e.g., clinical BioBERT, general T5), perform a concrete task like information extraction or question-answering from paired text snippets. The agents' interaction logs—including agreement rates, conflict resolution steps, and confidence scores—will be used to derive a novel similarity metric. We will validate this metric against existing STS benchmarks (e.g., STS-B, clinical NLI datasets) and, crucially, through correlation with performance on external, labeled clinical tasks (e.g., bladder cancer cohort identification), as per Mihalcea's recent work on evaluation in the wild.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute 1) a novel, task-driven methodology for STS evaluation that reduces reliance on direct human judgments, 2) an open-source multi-agent framework for generating intrinsic evaluation signals, and 3) new insights into the relationship between semantic similarity and performance on downstream clinical NLP tasks.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in shifting the paradigm from regressing to human scores to deriving similarity from the collaborative dynamics of a multi-agent system performing a concrete task, directly addressing the 'Agent Paradox' of simulating complex behavior for evaluation.

proposals.title: Beyond Equivalence: A Multi-Agent Framework for Granular Semantic Dissimilarity Scoring in Clinical Language
proposals.motivation: Current NLP evaluation and clinical information extraction heavily focus on semantic equivalence or similarity (e.g., NLI, STS). However, critical tasks like detecting contradictions in patient records, flagging novel or unexpected clinical findings, and evaluating the divergence of AI-generated text from trusted sources require a nuanced understanding of semantic *dissimilarity*. Pre-2026 methods lack dedicated, fine-grained models for scoring and characterizing the nature of divergence, treating it merely as the absence of similarity.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a multi-agent system, where specialized agents are trained to identify distinct *types* of semantic divergence (e.g., contradiction, irrelevance, novel information, paraphrase-with-difference), will significantly outperform monolithic LLMs and similarity-focused models in accurately scoring and describing semantic dissimilarity in clinical narratives.
proposals.method: We will develop a multi-agent framework where each agent is a fine-tuned LLM (e.g., ClinicalBERT, BioMedLM) specialized via contrastive learning on a labeled dataset of clinical text pairs annotated for specific divergence descriptors (e.g., 'contradicts', 'adds detail', 'unrelated'). A meta-agent, informed by Mihalcea's work on performance descriptors, will orchestrate these specialists, aggregating their confidence scores into a final dissimilarity score and a descriptive label. The system will be evaluated on a held-out test set from the variability-in-bladder-cancer study and a novel 'in-the-wild' dataset of clinician-AI interaction logs.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute 1) a novel benchmark and evaluation framework for semantic dissimilarity, moving beyond binary or similarity-centric metrics; 2) a publicly available, multi-agent system for granular divergence scoring; and 3) new insights into the failure modes of LLMs in clinical contexts by systematically categorizing how and where their outputs diverge from source material.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in explicitly framing and modeling semantic *dissimilarity* as a primary, structured task—rather than a byproduct of similarity failure—and in using a descriptor-driven, multi-agent architecture to decompose and explain it, which is a departure from single-model NLI/STS approaches prevalent pre-2026.

proposals.title: Cross-Modal Alignment Transfer: Leveraging Vision-Language Models for Fine-Grained Text-Text Semantic Similarity in Clinical Narratives
proposals.motivation: Current methods for measuring semantic similarity between clinical text passages (e.g., patient notes, research criteria) often rely on general-purpose LLM embeddings or task-specific fine-tuning, which can struggle with nuanced, domain-specific meaning. This is critical for applications like aligning patient records with trial protocols or summarizing medical histories, where performance directly impacts clinical decision support. A novel approach is needed to move beyond coarse similarity scores to capture fine-grained, descriptor-level semantic alignment.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that contrastive learning objectives and alignment architectures from state-of-the-art image-text models (e.g., CLIP, ALIGN) can be effectively transferred to the text-text modality to learn superior, disentangled representations for fine-grained clinical semantic similarity, outperforming standard sentence-embedding methods.
proposals.method: We will adapt the dual-encoder contrastive framework of models like CLIP, replacing the image encoder with a second text encoder. Using labeled clinical text pairs (e.g., from the NLI or STS-B benchmarks adapted with medical terminology, or from existing labeled datasets of bladder cancer notes), we will train the system using a symmetric cross-entropy loss on paired and unpaired text snippets. Specific architectures will include initializing encoders from clinical LLMs (like BioBERT or ClinicalBERT) and experimenting with token-level alignment losses (inspired by multi-modal grounding) to capture descriptor-level correspondence, not just sentence-level similarity.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute: 1) a novel method for text-text semantic similarity that leverages cross-modal alignment principles, 2) a systematic evaluation framework (extending Mihalcea's prior work on 'in-the-wild' evaluation) to assess the robustness and clinical utility of the learned representations, and 3) new benchmarks or analyses on the variability of alignment techniques for extracting precise clinical information.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in directly transferring core technical machinery from cross-modal (vision-language) alignment to a unimodal (text-text) task, a non-obvious transfer given the different data modalities, and in applying this to the high-stakes, descriptor-rich domain of clinical language where fine-grained alignment is paramount.

proposals.title: DUAL: A Controllable Generation Framework for Specified Semantic Similarity in Clinical Language
proposals.motivation: In clinical NLP, generating text variants with precise semantic fidelity is crucial for data augmentation, de-identification, and patient-facing communication. Current LLM-based generation offers limited, coarse-grained control over output similarity, risking loss of critical clinical descriptors or introduction of unsafe content. A system that can reliably produce text at a target similarity level would enable rigorous, in-the-wild evaluation of AI systems and support safer multi-agent clinical simulations.
proposals.hypothesis: By integrating a similarity feedback loop into a controllable text generation architecture, we can train a system to reliably produce output text that meets a pre-specified semantic similarity target to an input source text.
proposals.method: We propose DUAL (Dual-Objective Augmentation for Language), a two-stage system. First, we fine-tune a T5 or GPT-2 model on a clinical corpus (e.g., MIMIC-III notes) using a multi-task objective: generating the original text and a labeled, perturbed version (e.g., paraphrased, summarized). Second, we integrate a similarity controller module, using a pre-trained sentence encoder (e.g., SBERT) to compute cosine similarity, which provides reward signals for a reinforcement learning fine-tuning stage (e.g., PPO) where the action is text generation and the reward is based on proximity to a target similarity score.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide: 1) a novel framework and benchmark for controllable similarity text generation, 2) an analysis of the trade-offs between similarity, fluency, and clinical accuracy in generated text, and 3) an open-source tool for creating calibrated text variants to stress-test the robustness of clinical information extraction systems.
proposals.novelty: While prior work focuses on maximizing similarity (e.g., paraphrasing) or divergence (e.g., style transfer), DUAL explicitly targets an arbitrary, user-specified point on the similarity spectrum. This moves beyond binary 'faithful/unfaithful' generation, enabling nuanced control critical for clinical and evaluative applications.

proposals.title: Semantic Retrieval in the Wild: A Multi-Agent, Descriptor-Based Framework for Robust Clinical Text Similarity
proposals.motivation: Accurate semantic retrieval of clinical text snippets is critical for tasks like cohort identification, evidence synthesis, and decision support, but existing methods often fail in real-world settings due to variability in language, noise, and the need for domain-specific understanding. This problem is exacerbated by the documented variability of LLMs in extracting structured clinical information, highlighting the need for robust, generalizable retrieval systems that perform reliably outside controlled benchmarks.
proposals.hypothesis: A multi-agent retrieval system, where specialized agents generate diverse contextual descriptors for both query and corpus documents, will yield more robust and clinically relevant semantic similarity matches than monolithic embedding models, especially for noisy, unstructured medical text.
proposals.method: We will develop a multi-agent system where distinct, fine-tuned agents (e.g., for summarizing, extracting medical entities, generating lay descriptions) process text to produce multiple descriptor vectors per snippet. For retrieval, we will compute similarity between a query and corpus snippet using a late-fusion mechanism (e.g., a learned weighted combination) over the similarities of their respective descriptor sets. The system will be trained and evaluated on labeled clinical note pairs (e.g., from the MIMIC-III corpus) and tested on the noisy bladder cancer dataset from prior work to assess robustness.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel, interpretable framework for semantic retrieval that leverages multi-agent specialization to improve robustness in noisy domains. It will also provide a new evaluation paradigm, extending Mihalcea's 'in the wild' framework, to assess retrieval systems under realistic conditions of linguistic and domain variability.
proposals.novelty: The approach is novel in applying a multi-agent architecture, inspired by the 'Agent Paradox' inquiry, to the core retrieval problem, moving beyond single-vector embeddings. It uniquely uses descriptor diversity as a mechanism to counteract LLM instability and domain shift in clinical language.

proposals.title: DiscourseMRF: A Markov Random Field Framework for Modeling and Evaluating Discourse Coherence
proposals.motivation: Despite advances in LLMs, their text generation and evaluation often treat sentences as independent units, ignoring the structured, relational nature of coherent discourse. This limits performance in clinical and other high-stakes domains where logical flow and argument consistency are critical. Current methods lack a principled, energy-based model to capture global dependencies between sentences, analogous to how MRFs model pixel dependencies for image segmentation.
proposals.hypothesis: Modeling a document as a Markov Random Field, with sentences as nodes and rhetorical relations as edges, will provide a more accurate and interpretable model of discourse coherence than sentence-isolated or sequential n-gram approaches. This structured energy-based model will improve both the automatic evaluation of text coherence and the generation of coherent multi-sentence sequences.
proposals.method: We will construct a Conditional Random Field (CRF) over a discourse graph. Unary potentials will be derived from fine-tuned BERT or clinical BERT models to capture sentence content. Pairwise potentials will encode coherence constraints, combining features for entity grid transitions, discourse markers (RST/Penn Discourse Treebank relations), and pragmatic cues. For inference, we will adapt efficient loopy belief propagation or graph-cut algorithms. The framework will be trained and evaluated on labeled clinical narratives (e.g., MIMIC-III notes) and multi-turn dialogue datasets.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel, interpretable probabilistic framework for discourse analysis that explicitly models inter-sentence dependencies. It will yield a new metric for automated coherence evaluation that correlates better with human judgments. The model will also serve as a structured prior or reranker to improve the discourse coherence of LLM-generated text in clinical and conversational systems.
proposals.novelty: The direct adaptation of MRF/CRF energy-based models from computer vision to the discourse coherence problem is novel, moving beyond common sequential or graph-neural-network approaches. It uniquely combines established linguistic discourse theories with robust probabilistic graphical models for global inference over text structure.

proposals.title: FactCheck-Fuse: A Hybrid Summarization System with Extractive Fallback for Factual Consistency in Clinical Narratives
proposals.motivation: Abstractive summarization systems, particularly LLM-based ones, are prone to generating factually inconsistent 'hallucinations,' a critical failure in high-stakes domains like clinical documentation. While recent work has explored post-hoc factuality verification, integrating verification as a core, fail-safe component of the generation pipeline remains underexplored. This gap is especially pertinent given the documented variability of LLMs in extracting clinical information, necessitating systems that guarantee factual grounding.
proposals.hypothesis: A hybrid summarization system that constrains abstractive fusion with a factuality verification layer and defaults to verified extractive sentences when confidence is low will produce summaries that are significantly more factually consistent than purely abstractive models, without sacrificing fluency in cases where safe abstraction is possible.
proposals.method: We propose a modular pipeline: 1) An extractor (e.g., BERT-based sentence ranker) identifies salient source sentences. 2) A fusion module (a fine-tuned T5 or BART model) generates abstractive paraphrases of these ranked spans. 3) A verification layer, inspired by recent 'question generation and answering' (QG-QA) factuality metrics, checks each abstractive sentence against the source for entity and relation consistency. 4) A decision module, using the verifier's confidence score, selects the abstractive fusion or defaults to the original extractive sentence. The system will be trained and evaluated on clinical datasets like MIMIC-III and the bladder cancer notes from prior work.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel, practically deployable architecture for trustworthy text summarization that explicitly prioritizes factual correctness. It will provide a new evaluation framework for measuring the factuality-fluency trade-off 'in the wild,' extending Mihalcea's prior evaluation work. The resulting system and analysis will offer concrete guidelines for building reliable NLP systems in sensitive, descriptor-rich domains.
proposals.novelty: The core novelty is the tight integration of verification as a *control mechanism* within the generation loop, triggering a fallback to extraction—a form of adversarial robustness against the model's own hallucinations. This moves beyond post-hoc evaluation or simple prompting, creating a self-correcting multi-stage system.

proposals.title: Learning to Weigh: Data-Driven Optimization of Rhetorical Network Edge Weights for Clinical Narrative Prediction
proposals.motivation: Network models of discourse, where nodes are propositions and edges are rhetorical relations (e.g., ELABORATION, CONTRAST), offer a structured view of clinical narratives. However, their predictive power for downstream tasks like outcome prediction is limited by reliance on intuitive, uniform edge weights, which fail to capture the nuanced, asymmetric influence of different relation types on information flow. A data-driven method to infer these weights is needed to move discourse analysis from descriptive modeling to robust, task-oriented prediction, directly addressing the challenge of extracting reliable clinical information from LLM-processed text noted in recent work.
proposals.hypothesis: Optimal, asymmetric weights for rhetorical relation types in a discourse network can be learned from data to maximize performance on a clinical prediction task, and these learned weights will reveal interpretable patterns of discourse influence that outperform heuristic baselines.
proposals.method: We will construct rhetorical networks from clinical notes (e.g., bladder cancer records) using a pre-2026 discourse parser (like DPLP or an LLM prompted for RST relations). We treat the adjacency matrix of relation types as a parameterized, weighted graph. Using a graph neural network (GNN) as a differentiable proxy, we will optimize the edge-type weight parameters via backpropagation against a supervised objective (e.g., readmission prediction). The optimization framework will use a multi-agent reinforcement learning-inspired meta-learner, where one 'agent' proposes weight configurations and another evaluates network performance, creating an adversarial search for optimal schemes, aligning with Mihalcea's recent methods.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first reproducible, objective framework for inferring rhetorical edge weights from task performance, shifting discourse analysis from intuition to empirical grounding. It will yield a novel, weighted discourse network model with demonstrated utility for clinical NLP tasks. The resulting weight functions will also offer interpretable insights into which rhetorical moves are most salient for specific predictive contexts.
proposals.novelty: While graph weights are often learned for node features, learning them for edge *types* (rhetorical relations) from global task performance is novel. The approach combines discourse structure with network science optimization in a way that directly addresses the 'Agent Paradox' by using a simplified multi-agent system to automate a complex parameter search typically done by human intuition.

proposals.title: Cascaded, Discourse-Aware Sentence Graph Construction for Robust Clinical Narrative Understanding
proposals.motivation: Extracting structured information from clinical narratives, such as electronic health records, is a critical NLP task for downstream applications like cohort identification and outcome prediction. Current methods often rely on monolithic LLM calls or all-pair semantic similarity, which is computationally expensive, opaque, and can miss nuanced discourse relations (e.g., causality, temporal order) that are crucial for accurate interpretation. A robust, efficient, and interpretable system for modeling sentence connectivity is needed for reliable deployment in clinical settings.
proposals.hypothesis: A multi-stage, cascaded architecture that explicitly models different link types (co-reference, explicit discourse, deep semantic) with specialized, low-parameter filters will construct more accurate and interpretable sentence connectivity graphs for clinical text than a monolithic LLM-based approach, while being significantly more computationally efficient.
proposals.method: We propose a three-stage pipeline. First, a fast rule-based and statistical co-reference resolver (e.g., spaCy) and key concept matcher prunes candidate links. Second, a battery of lightweight, linear or shallow neural classifiers, trained on PDTB-style relations, labels explicit discourse markers (e.g., 'because', 'however') on the remaining candidates. Third, for candidates without explicit markers, a contextualized similarity model (e.g., a fine-tuned ClinicalBERT or BioBERT) is applied, but its input is enriched with the discourse context and the outputs of the previous stage's classifiers. The final graph is assembled from these filtered, typed links.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a novel, efficient system for discourse-aware sentence linking tailored for clinical narratives, directly addressing the variability and cost issues highlighted in recent LLM-based clinical IE studies. It will provide a new evaluation framework for structured text understanding 'in the wild,' focusing on robustness and interpretability over raw performance, advancing towards more reliable clinical NLP systems.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the cascaded, hybrid architecture that strategically combines fast symbolic filters with targeted deep learning, explicitly separating relation types to prevent over-reliance on a single black-box similarity score. This moves beyond standard multi-agent or pure LLM approaches by designing a specialized 'system' of lightweight, interpretable components.

proposals.title: Coherence-Aware Graph Representations for Clinical Narrative Understanding
proposals.motivation: Current methods for modeling clinical narratives in LLMs often rely on bag-of-words semantics or simple similarity, failing to capture the crucial discourse structure (e.g., causality, elaboration) that conveys diagnostic reasoning and patient progression. This limits performance in downstream tasks like information extraction from complex medical records and question answering, where the relationship between facts is as important as the facts themselves. A discourse-aware model is needed to move beyond local semantics to global coherence.
proposals.hypothesis: Explicitly modeling predicted discourse relations as weighted constraints on a semantic graph will yield representations that better distinguish texts with similar content but different rhetorical structures, leading to improved performance on clinical NLP tasks requiring narrative understanding.
proposals.method: We will construct a two-stream graph encoder. The first stream uses a fine-tuned clinical LLM (e.g., BioBERT, ClinicalBERT) to generate node embeddings and compute semantic similarity edges. The second stream uses a pre-2026 discourse parser (e.g., based on PDTB relations) to predict rhetorical relations (e.g., CONTRAST, RESULT) between text spans, producing discourse edge weights. A gating mechanism will fuse these edge types into a unified adjacency matrix. This graph will be processed by a Graph Neural Network (GNN) and evaluated on labeled clinical datasets for tasks like timeline reconstruction and outcome prediction from bladder cancer notes.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel, interpretable framework for integrating discourse and semantics into a single graph model, directly applicable to complex clinical narratives. It will advance methods for evaluating AI systems 'in the wild' by offering a finer-grained analysis of model coherence. The resulting representations will enable more accurate and reliable extraction of clinical information from unstructured text.
proposals.novelty: Unlike prior work that treats discourse and semantics separately, this approach jointly learns their interaction within a unified graph structure, using discourse to explicitly constrain and interpret semantic similarity, which is novel for clinical text modeling.

proposals.title: Semantic Graph Construction for Clinical Narrative Coherence: A Multi-Agent Evaluation Framework
proposals.motivation: Current methods for building sentence connectivity graphs in clinical narratives, such as for timeline extraction or summarization, often rely on simplistic lexical overlap. This fails to capture the rich semantic relationships (e.g., causality, temporal sequence, symptom-procedure links) crucial for accurate clinical understanding. As LLMs are increasingly deployed in clinical information extraction, a robust, semantically-grounded linking method is needed to improve downstream task performance and system reliability in real-world settings.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that replacing word-overlap-based sentence linking with a multi-faceted semantic similarity measure, evaluated within a structured multi-agent framework, will yield more coherent and clinically accurate narrative graphs, thereby improving the performance of downstream tasks like timeline reconstruction and summarization.
proposals.method: We will develop a system where a 'Descriptor' agent uses a clinical-domain fine-tuned language model (e.g., BioBERT, ClinicalBERT) to generate dense sentence embeddings. A 'Linker' agent will construct a graph using a composite similarity score combining these embeddings (via cosine similarity) with structured clinical descriptor matches (e.g., UMLS concepts). This graph will be evaluated by a separate 'Evaluator' agent within a simulated user-in-the-loop framework, assessing coherence against expert-labeled clinical timelines for bladder cancer narratives, extending methods from Mihalcea's prior work on variability in LLM extraction.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute 1) a novel, semantically-informed graph construction method for clinical narratives, 2) a practical evaluation framework integrating multi-agent roles (descriptor, linker, evaluator) to assess system components in 'the wild', and 3) new benchmark data with labeled semantic links for clinical text, advancing the field beyond lexical matching.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the integration of deep semantic similarity into a dynamic, multi-agent graph construction system specifically designed for the clinical domain, coupled with an in-situ evaluation framework that moves beyond static benchmarks to assess real-world utility and coherence.

proposals.title: EmoGraph: Learning Adaptive Graph Structures for Nuanced Emotion Dynamics in Clinical and Social Text
proposals.motivation: Current NLP models for emotion analysis rely on static, pre-defined categories and co-occurrence thresholds, failing to capture the complex, blended, and domain-specific nature of emotional expression. This is a critical limitation for analyzing mental health discourse, clinical narratives, and affective storytelling, where understanding the nuanced dynamics and transitions between emotional states is essential for accurate interpretation and application.
proposals.hypothesis: An adaptive graph construction framework that learns context-sensitive parameters for linking and weighting fine-grained emotional states directly from data will more accurately model the unique emotional landscape and transition patterns of a specific text corpus than fixed-threshold or predefined-graph approaches.
proposals.method: We propose a two-stage neural architecture. First, a fine-tuned LLM (e.g., Llama 2, Mistral) or a multi-task model extracts a dense, continuous representation of fine-grained emotional states (e.g., Plutchik's wheel blends) from text spans. Second, a Graph Neural Network (GNN) with a learnable structure learning component (e.g., using neural message passing or a latent variable model) ingests these representations and iteratively refines an adjacency matrix, optimizing edge existence and weights for a downstream task (e.g., narrative arc classification or clinical outcome prediction). This allows the graph topology—what emotions are linked and how strongly—to emerge from the corpus.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide: 1) a novel, data-driven framework for modeling emotion dynamics as adaptive graphs, 2) a new benchmark for evaluating emotion transition modeling across domains (clinical, social media, literature), and 3) a powerful analytical tool for revealing latent emotional structures in mental health and narrative analysis, advancing beyond static emotion classification.
proposals.novelty: The core novelty is replacing fixed, heuristic rules for constructing emotion graphs with a learned, context-sensitive parameterization, enabling the discovery of corpus-specific emotional syntax rather than imposing a universal one.

proposals.title: Semantic Restart Random Walks: A Neural Relevance-Guided Framework for Robust Query-Focused Summarization
proposals.motivation: Query-focused summarization is critical for clinical information retrieval, where practitioners need concise, relevant answers from lengthy patient records. Pre-2026 methods often rely on lexical overlap or static graph structures, which fail under vocabulary mismatch and complex semantic intents. This limits the real-world utility of AI systems in high-stakes domains like healthcare, as highlighted by recent work on LLM variability in extracting clinical data.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that dynamically generating the restart distribution in a random walk with restart algorithm using a neural relevance model will produce summaries with superior semantic alignment to the query intent compared to keyword-based or static graph methods.
proposals.method: We will implement a two-stage system. First, a fine-tuned transformer encoder (e.g., ClinicalBERT or DeBERTa) will compute a semantic relevance score for each document sentence relative to the query. These scores form a probability distribution for restart nodes in a sentence similarity graph. Second, we will perform a Personalized PageRank (PPR) random walk on this graph, where the walker restarts at a sentence with probability proportional to its neural relevance score. The stationary distribution of this walk provides the final sentence ranking for summary extraction.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel, robust algorithm for query-focused summarization that is less sensitive to lexical gaps. It will provide an evaluation framework for assessing semantic alignment in summarization systems deployed 'in the wild,' such as in clinical settings. The method also offers an interpretable bridge between deep neural relevance models and established graph-based ranking algorithms.
proposals.novelty: The key novelty is the dynamic, neural generation of the restart distribution for PPR, moving beyond pre-defined query nodes or uniform restarts. This integrates deep semantic understanding directly into the graph walk's stationary properties, a non-obvious fusion of representation learning and spectral graph theory for summarization.

proposals.title: Belief Propagation on Multi-Resource Semantic Graphs for Contextualized and Explainable Emotion Inference
proposals.motivation: Accurate emotion inference from text is critical for clinical applications like mental health monitoring and patient interaction analysis. However, existing methods often rely on single lexical resources or black-box LLMs, which lack explainability and struggle to resolve contextual ambiguity. A principled, probabilistic framework that integrates multiple, complementary emotion knowledge bases is needed for robust, interpretable predictions in real-world settings.
proposals.hypothesis: A belief propagation algorithm operating on a heterogeneous graph that connects text units to emotion concepts from multiple structured resources (e.g., SenticNet, NRC, FrameNet) will yield more accurate and contextually coherent emotion inferences than single-resource or end-to-end neural baselines, while providing explicit reasoning paths.
proposals.method: We will construct a factor graph where nodes represent text tokens and emotion labels from integrated resources (SenticNet, NRC Emotion Lexicon, FrameNet, WordNet-Affect), connected by edges based on syntactic dependencies and semantic relations. A message-passing algorithm (e.g., Loopy Belief Propagation) will propagate and marginalize belief scores across the graph. Contextual consistency will be modeled through compatibility potentials between emotion nodes, and final inference will weight evidence from each resource based on the strength of relational paths and node agreement.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a novel, explainable framework for emotion inference that probabilistically fuses heterogeneous lexical knowledge. It will advance the field by providing a transparent alternative to opaque LLM-based methods, particularly valuable for clinical and social computing domains where interpretability is paramount. The resulting system and annotated multi-resource benchmark will be publicly released.
proposals.novelty: Unlike prior graph-based emotion models that use a single resource or treat resources as separate features, this approach formulates multi-resource fusion as a structured probabilistic inference problem on a unified semantic graph, enabling dynamic, context-sensitive evidence weighting.

proposals.title: Goal-Conditioned Micro-Text Abstraction: A Framework for Context-Aware Utility in Short Text Processing
proposals.motivation: Current text summarization models, especially for very short texts like social media posts, clinical notes, or system logs, often produce generic compressions that lose task-specific utility. In real-world applications, the value of a text snippet depends on the user's goal, such as information triage for a clinician or content repackaging for a social media manager. There is a critical need for systems that can dynamically reshape micro-texts into actionable abstractions based on explicit contextual goals.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a model trained to first classify the discourse function of a micro-text and then generate a goal-conditioned abstraction will produce outputs with significantly higher contextual utility and consistency than standard compression or generic summarization models.
proposals.method: We will develop a two-stage encoder-decoder transformer framework. First, a classifier module, trained on a newly annotated dataset of micro-texts, will label the text's primary discourse function (e.g., assertion, query, instruction). This label will be concatenated with a user-specified goal descriptor (e.g., 'for database indexing', 'for patient alert') and the original text as input to a T5 or BART-based generator. The model will be trained using a multi-task objective combining reconstruction loss and a novel adversarial loss, where a discriminator evaluates if the abstraction is useful for the specified goal, inspired by recent work on evaluation frameworks for AI 'in the wild'.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute: 1) a novel framework and benchmark dataset for goal-conditioned micro-text abstraction, 2) a new adversarial training method for optimizing contextual utility, and 3) empirical validation in domains like clinical information extraction and cross-platform content repackaging, extending research on LLM variability in clinical settings.
proposals.novelty: Unlike standard summarization, our approach explicitly conditions the abstraction on an external utility goal and integrates a diagnostic discourse analysis step, creating a more interpretable and controllable pipeline for transforming minimal text into contextually useful representations.

proposals.title: Semantic Codecs for Micro-Text: A Signal Compression Approach to Ultra-Short Summarization
proposals.motivation: Current summarization models are optimized for documents, performing poorly on micro-texts (e.g., tweets, headlines, clinical notes) where redundancy is low and every token is critical. This gap limits applications in real-time information feeds, constrained communication channels, and clinical data extraction where concise, information-dense output is required. Drawing inspiration from specialized audio codecs like Speex, which use psychoacoustic models to compress short speech signals by preserving only perceptually critical components, we posit that NLP needs analogous 'semantic codecs' for micro-text.
proposals.hypothesis: A transformer-based model, trained with a novel objective function derived from signal compression principles, can act as a semantic compressor for texts under 50 words, identifying and preserving core semantic content (entities, primary relation, intent) with extreme token efficiency while discarding syntactically predictable or pragmatically redundant elements.
proposals.method: We will design a multi-task training objective combining reconstruction loss (for fidelity) with a novel 'semantic sparsity' loss, penalizing the retention of tokens predictable from a base language model. The architecture will be a compact encoder-decoder Transformer, trained on a large, labeled corpus of (micro-text, ultra-summary) pairs. We will adapt evaluation frameworks from Mihalcea's prior work on AI systems 'in the wild' and clinical information extraction, creating a rigorous benchmark assessing compression ratio, factual retention (via entity/relation extraction), and user-rated informativeness.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a novel model and training paradigm for a neglected but critical summarization task, advancing the field of efficient language generation. It will provide a new evaluation framework for micro-summarization and demonstrate the utility of cross-disciplinary analogies from signal processing to NLP. The resulting 'semantic codec' has direct applications in real-time social media monitoring, user interface design, and structured data extraction from brief clinical texts.
proposals.novelty: The approach is novel in its direct conceptual translation of audio compression techniques (e.g., psychoacoustic modeling) to semantic compression, moving beyond standard sequence-to-sequence summarization. The proposed 'semantic sparsity' loss and the explicit focus on the sub-50-word domain constitute a significant departure from existing document-focused summarization research.

proposals.title: Grounded Text Expansion for Clinical Narratives: A Graph-Retrieval and Multi-Agent Verification Framework
proposals.motivation: Large language models (LLMs) can generate fluent text expansions but often produce ungrounded or contradictory information, a critical flaw in high-stakes domains like clinical documentation. Existing retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) systems primarily use graphs for retrieval but lack rigorous, post-retrieval verification of semantic coherence and factual accuracy against trusted sources. This gap limits the reliability of automated text expansion for tasks like enriching sparse medical notes.
proposals.hypothesis: A pipeline that first retrieves candidate facts from a structured knowledge graph and then subjects them to a multi-agent verification process against a trusted corpus will produce text expansions with significantly higher factual accuracy and semantic coherence than standard graph-RAG or LLM-only approaches.
proposals.method: We propose a three-stage system: 1) Graph-Guided Retrieval: Use a biomedical knowledge graph (e.g., UMLS) with graph traversal algorithms to retrieve candidate entities and relationships for a seed clinical text. 2) Multi-Agent Verification: Implement a lightweight multi-agent system where specialized verifier agents (e.g., for consistency, relevance, support) query a trusted corpus (e.g., clinical guidelines, labeled medical records) to score each candidate. This adapts Mihalcea's 'in the wild' evaluation philosophy into the generation loop. 3) Grounded Generation: A final generator LLM integrates only high-confidence, verified candidates into a coherent expansion, with the verification scores used as constraints.
proposals.contribution: This work contributes a novel pipeline for trustworthy text expansion that tightly integrates symbolic graph reasoning with neural verification. It provides a new benchmark and framework for evaluating grounded generation in clinical NLP. The study will also yield insights into the 'agent paradox' by examining if simple, specialized agents can effectively collaborate to enforce coherence where a single LLM fails.
proposals.novelty: The key novelty is the formal, multi-faceted verification stage that treats the knowledge graph as a hypothesis generator rather than a direct knowledge source, actively testing retrieved propositions against a ground-truth corpus before integration. This moves beyond using graphs purely for retrieval.

proposals.title: Semantic Mass Conservation: A Graph-Based Constraint for Coherent and Faithful Text Expansion
proposals.motivation: Current LLM-based text expansion often introduces hallucinated or irrelevant content, limiting reliability in high-stakes domains like clinical documentation. There is a need for generation methods that are both creative and strictly faithful to a source's semantic core. Drawing an analogy to fluid dynamics, where mass conservation constrains flow field reconstruction from sparse data, offers a principled framework for this control problem.
proposals.hypothesis: Treating a knowledge graph as a semantic flow field and enforcing a conservation principle over core concepts during graph-walk-based generation will produce expanded text that is more coherent, relevant, and faithful to the seed text than unconstrained LLM expansion.
proposals.method: We will formalize the 'Semantic Mass Conservation' constraint by representing the seed text as a set of concept nodes in a knowledge graph (e.g., ConceptNet, UMLS for clinical text). Using a multi-agent system architecture, one agent will map the seed to the graph, a second will perform constrained random walks to identify semantically conserved expansion pathways, and a third, a fine-tuned LLM (e.g., Llama 2, GPT-4), will generate sentences conditioned on these pathways. The constraint is enforced by requiring all generated content to be reachable via graph edges from seed concepts, with a 'mass' score tracking concept coverage.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute 1) a novel, interpretable framework for controlled text generation grounded in a cross-disciplinary analogy, 2) a new evaluation benchmark for faithfulness in text expansion, particularly for clinical narratives, and 3) a multi-agent system that operationalizes semantic constraints for reliable in-the-wild AI applications.
proposals.novelty: The core novelty is the adaptation of a physical conservation law as a formal constraint for NLP, moving beyond probabilistic or prompt-based control. This differs from existing graph-to-text methods by using the graph not as a rigid template but as a field governing generative interpolation.

proposals.title: Controlled Text Expansion via Graph-Guided Multi-Agent Systems for Clinical Information Elaboration
proposals.motivation: Current NLP focuses heavily on text summarization and information extraction, but the inverse problem—intelligently expanding a concise seed text with relevant, explanatory information—is understudied. This is critical in clinical and educational domains where a short note (e.g., a diagnosis or a performance descriptor) must be elaborated for different audiences, ensuring completeness and mitigating the variability of single LLM outputs. An evaluation framework for such generative systems 'in the wild' is also needed.
proposals.hypothesis: A multi-agent system, guided by a knowledge graph of clinical concepts and explanatory relations, can generate more accurate, relevant, and controllable explanatory text expansions from a short seed than a monolithic LLM, reducing output variability and hallucination.
proposals.method: We will construct a knowledge graph from labeled clinical corpora (e.g., MIMIC-III) where nodes are medical concepts and edges represent explanatory relations (e.g., 'symptom_of', 'treated_by'). A multi-agent system will be designed where specialized agents (e.g., for concept identification, relation retrieval, sentence formulation) operate over this graph. The 'Concept Agent' will parse the seed text, the 'Retrieval Agent' will traverse the graph for relevant connected concepts and template sentences, and a 'Formulation Agent' (an LLM like GPT-4 or Llama 2) will synthesize fluent expansions. A final 'Verification Agent' will check against the graph.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel framework for controlled text generation, moving beyond summarization to reliable expansion. It will contribute a new evaluation benchmark for text expansion in the clinical domain, measuring factual consistency, relevance, and explanatory utility. The multi-agent design offers a transparent and modular alternative to end-to-end LLMs for sensitive applications.
proposals.novelty: The approach inverts the standard text compression task to focus on graph-guided expansion. It uniquely combines Mihalcea's work on multi-agent systems and clinical NLP with graph-based knowledge representation to control LLM generation, addressing the 'Agent Paradox' by using specialized agents to mimic structured human explanatory reasoning.

proposals.title: Multi-Scale Spectral Graph Ranking for Context-Aware Clinical Text Summarization
proposals.motivation: Current LLM-based methods for extracting and ranking information from clinical text, such as medical records, often produce flat, context-insensitive outputs. This is problematic for complex narratives where the importance of a textual unit (e.g., a symptom mention) depends on its conceptual context across sentences and paragraphs. A method that captures the hierarchical, multi-scale conceptual structure of text is needed for more nuanced clinical analysis.
proposals.hypothesis: By modeling text as a multi-resolution semantic graph and applying hierarchical spectral clustering techniques from brain network analysis, we can identify stable conceptual communities across scales, leading to a more contextually accurate ranking of textual units for summarization and information extraction.
proposals.method: We will construct a multi-layer graph where nodes are textual units at different scales (sentences, paragraphs, LLM-extracted clinical concepts). Edges within each layer will be weighted by semantic similarity (using pre-2026 sentence-BERT embeddings). We will adapt the multi-scale community detection framework of Lancichinetti et al. (2009, 2011), using a hierarchical spectral clustering approach with a resolution parameter to optimize a stability function across scales. Node importance will be computed via a multi-scale version of eigenvector centrality, integrating centrality within a local cluster and connectivity to other clusters.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide: 1) a novel graph-based framework for hierarchical text analysis that moves beyond flat rankings, 2) a new evaluation methodology for 'in-the-wild' clinical AI systems that assesses performance across conceptual scales, and 3) an open-source tool for multi-scale clinical note summarization that accounts for contextual importance.
proposals.novelty: The direct adaptation of multi-resolution modularity optimization from computational neuroscience to NLP for ranking is novel, as is the explicit generation of a hierarchical importance ranking tied to stable conceptual communities rather than a single global score.

proposals.title: Beyond the Scalar: A Multi-Aspect Descriptor Framework for Evaluating Clinical Language Model Outputs
proposals.motivation: Current evaluation of LLMs in clinical NLP, such as information extraction from medical records, often relies on single scalar similarity scores (e.g., BERTScore, ROUGE). This collapses critical semantic dimensions—like factual correctness, clinical relevance, temporal consistency, and patient safety implication—into one number, obscuring the specific nature of errors. This is a major limitation for deploying AI systems in the wild, where understanding failure modes is as crucial as measuring aggregate performance.
proposals.hypothesis: Replacing a single similarity score with a multi-dimensional vector of interpretable semantic descriptors will provide a more accurate, diagnostic, and actionable evaluation of LLM-generated clinical text, better correlating with expert human judgment of utility and risk.
proposals.method: We will develop a multi-agent evaluation system where specialized agents, each fine-tuned on clinical corpora, act as aspect-specific critics. For a given LLM output and reference text, one agent will score factual overlap, another will assess clinical coherence using medical ontologies (e.g., UMLS), a third will check for hallucinated entities, and a fourth will evaluate pragmatic utility for a defined clinical task. These scores form a multi-aspect descriptor vector. We will validate the framework using the labeled dataset from bladder cancer records, correlating descriptor vectors with granular human annotations.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel evaluation framework that moves beyond monolithic scores to offer a diagnostic profile of LLM performance. It contributes a reusable, interpretable methodology for the clinical NLP community to better understand model strengths and weaknesses, directly addressing the 'Agent Paradox' by using specialized multi-agent systems to decompose a complex judgment task.
proposals.novelty: While multi-dimensional evaluation is known, applying a principled, multi-agent critic architecture to decompose semantic similarity into clinically meaningful, pre-defined aspects for LLM output evaluation is novel. This integrates Mihalcea's work on descriptors, clinical language, and multi-agent systems into a cohesive new evaluation paradigm.

proposals.title: Diagnosing the Language-Agnostic Illusion: A Multi-Agent, Multi-Lingual Framework for Probing Clinical Semantic Representations
proposals.motivation: The drive for language-agnostic semantic representations is central to scalable, equitable NLP, especially in clinical applications where information must be shared across linguistic boundaries. However, pre-2026 multilingual LLMs often exhibit performance disparities and may encode cultural or linguistic biases in their latent spaces, questioning the feasibility of true language-agnosticism. This is critical for deploying reliable AI systems in global healthcare settings, as seen in prior work on variability in clinical information extraction.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that while high-level clinical concepts can be aligned cross-lingually, language-specific syntactic structures and pragmatic nuances create irreducible 'semantic residues' that prevent a perfectly language-agnostic representation, measurable through systematic divergence in downstream task performance.
proposals.method: We will design a multi-agent system where each agent, fine-tuned on a different language's clinical corpus (e.g., English, Japanese, Spanish), engages in a collaborative diagnostic task requiring semantic understanding. Using contrastive learning (e.g., SimCSE) and probing classifiers, we will measure the alignment of hidden representations across languages for identical clinical intents. The framework will evaluate systems on labeled clinical descriptors from parallel medical records, analyzing performance gaps and inter-agent communication failures as evidence of non-agnostic semantic elements.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a rigorous, task-driven evaluation framework to quantify the limits of language-agnosticism in semantic representations, moving beyond intrinsic metrics. It will deliver a diagnostic toolkit for identifying which semantic aspects are lost in cross-lingual transfer, directly informing the development of more robust, linguistically-aware clinical NLP systems.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in combining the multi-agent paradigm—inspired by research on cognitive complexity—with fine-grained clinical semantic evaluation to operationalize and stress-test language-agnostic claims, moving from abstract analysis to performance-based, in-the-wild assessment.

proposals.title: Cross-Lingual Semantic Textual Similarity in the Wild: A Multi-Agent Evaluation Framework for Low-Resource and Clinical Language Pairs
proposals.motivation: Cross-lingual Semantic Textual Similarity (STS) is critical for global NLP applications, yet existing benchmarks and systems are heavily skewed towards high-resource European and Asian languages (e.g., English-Spanish, English-Japanese). This leaves a vast performance gap for low-resource languages and specialized domains like clinical text, where semantic equivalence is paramount for tasks like patient record matching or cross-border healthcare data analysis. A systematic evaluation framework is needed to assess the real-world robustness of models across this broader linguistic spectrum.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a multi-agent system, where specialized agents act as annotators, evaluators, and adversarial testers, will more accurately diagnose the failure modes and performance variability of state-of-the-art cross-lingual STS models across diverse language pairs than static benchmark evaluations.
proposals.method: We will construct a novel, multi-lingual STS corpus by extending existing clinical datasets (e.g., from bladder cancer notes) and general-domain parallel texts to include under-studied pairs (e.g., English-Swahili, English-Bengali, Japanese-Arabic). We will then implement a multi-agent evaluation framework inspired by recent work on system assessment. This framework will deploy LLM-based agents to: 1) generate and validate silver-standard cross-lingual paraphrase pairs, 2) execute a battery of tests measuring robustness to syntactic variation and domain shift, and 3) analyze error patterns. Models tested will include supervised sentence encoders (e.g., SBERT) and zero-shot LLMs (e.g., mT5, multilingual embeddings).
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute: 1) a new publicly available benchmark for cross-lingual STS covering clinical and low-resource language pairs, 2) a novel, dynamic multi-agent evaluation methodology for STS 'in the wild,' and 3) a detailed analysis of performance descriptors (e.g., syntactic distance, lexical overlap) that predict model success or failure across languages.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the fusion of a multi-agent, adversarial evaluation system—typically applied to broader AI system analysis—with the specific, fine-grained task of cross-lingual STS, and its application to a deliberately expanded set of language pairs that includes clinical domains and low-resource languages not covered by standard benchmarks like STS 2017.

proposals.title: Beyond Lexical Cues: A Multi-Agent Framework for Extracting Pragmatic and Semantic Descriptors from Clinical Language
proposals.motivation: Current LLM-based extraction from clinical notes, as shown in Mihalcea's recent work on bladder cancer, primarily relies on lexical and syntactic patterns, missing deeper patient context. This limits performance in real-world clinical systems where meaning depends on pragmatic factors like physician intent, patient history, and conversational implicature. A method to systematically model these features is needed to improve the reliability and nuance of AI in clinical decision support.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a multi-agent system, where specialized agents model distinct pragmatic and semantic phenomena (e.g., factuality, certainty, temporal anchoring), will extract more accurate and clinically relevant descriptors from unstructured medical records than monolithic LLM prompting.
proposals.method: We will design a multi-agent system where each agent is a fine-tuned or prompted LLM (e.g., LLaMA-2, GPT-3.5-Turbo) specialized for a specific linguistic feature: one for speech acts and directives, one for hedging and certainty, one for temporal reasoning, and one for coreference resolution of clinical entities. A central orchestrator agent, guided by a structured clinical ontology, will integrate these outputs. The framework will be evaluated on the bladder cancer dataset using the "in-the-wild" evaluation methodology from Mihalcea's prior work, comparing F1 scores and robustness against the baseline monolithic extraction.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute: 1) a novel, interpretable multi-agent architecture for deep linguistic feature extraction in clinical NLP, 2) a new labeled dataset annotating pragmatic features in clinical notes, and 3) empirical evidence on the value of pragmatic modeling for clinical information extraction performance and system reliability.
proposals.novelty: While multi-agent systems are being explored for cognition, their application to explicitly decompose and model pragmatic linguistic phenomena in clinical text is novel. This moves beyond using LLMs as monolithic extractors to using them as specialized, collaborative models of human clinical communication.

proposals.title: Deceptive Descriptors: A Multi-Agent Framework for Modeling Affective and Emotional Markers in Deceptive Language
proposals.motivation: Detecting deception in text is critical for applications in clinical settings (e.g., patient self-reports), security, and online communication. Current methods often rely on surface-level linguistic cues or generic LLM embeddings, lacking a nuanced model of the affective and emotional states that underpin deceptive writing. Grounded in pre-2026 work on clinical NLP and evaluation frameworks, this research addresses the need for robust, interpretable descriptors of deception in real-world, unconstrained text.
proposals.hypothesis: Deceptive language exhibits a distinct, quantifiable profile of affective and emotional markers (e.g., heightened negative emotion, reduced emotional specificity, increased cognitive load signals) that can be captured by transferring and adapting methods from fine-grained sentiment and emotion analysis.
proposals.method: We will construct a multi-agent system where specialized LLM-based agents (pre-trained, pre-2026 models like BERT or RoBERTa) are fine-tuned on labeled datasets for specific tasks: one for valence/arousal detection, one for fine-grained emotion classification (e.g., Ekman's), and one for cognitive descriptor extraction (e.g., certainty, hedging). These agents will analyze text in parallel, and their outputs will be integrated as a multi-dimensional descriptor vector. This framework will be evaluated on existing deception corpora (e.g., hotel reviews, clinical misinformation) and validated using Mihalcea's 'in the wild' evaluation principles to assess robustness and generalizability.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a novel, interpretable framework for deception detection that moves beyond binary classification to provide a rich profile of affective markers. It will contribute a new benchmark dataset with multi-dimensional affective labels for deceptive language and demonstrate the utility of a modular, multi-agent approach for modeling complex psychosocial constructs in NLP.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the systematic transfer of established sentiment/emotion analysis architectures into a coordinated multi-agent framework explicitly designed to model the affective substrate of deception, creating a bridge between well-studied affective computing tasks and the complex challenge of deceit detection.

proposals.title: Cross-Lingual Semantic Textual Similarity via Descriptor-Based Multi-Task Alignment
proposals.motivation: Semantic Textual Similarity (STS) is a core NLP task, but state-of-the-art models (e.g., based on fine-tuned BERT) are predominantly monolingual, requiring separate training and resources for each language. This limits their applicability in multilingual clinical and real-world settings, where information in medical records or user queries may appear in multiple languages, creating a barrier for equitable and scalable AI systems. A model that inherently understands semantic equivalence across languages without parallel sentence supervision is needed.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that by training a single model with a multi-task objective combining language-agnostic "descriptor" generation and contrastive alignment of multilingual embeddings, we can create an STS system that performs robustly on language pairs unseen during training.
proposals.method: We will leverage a pre-trained multilingual LLM (e.g., XLM-RoBERTa) as a backbone. The model will be trained on a combination of existing multilingual STS data (e.g., from SemEval) and clinical corpora (e.g., translated bladder cancer notes). We introduce a novel auxiliary task: for each input sentence, the model generates a language-invariant semantic "descriptor" vector. A multi-task loss will combine a standard STS regression loss with a contrastive loss that pulls descriptor vectors of semantically similar sentences (across languages) together and pushes dissimilar ones apart, enforcing cross-lingual alignment in the embedding space.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute 1) a novel, descriptor-augmented architecture for zero-shot cross-lingual STS, 2) a new evaluation framework for assessing STS models 'in the wild' on imbalanced, code-switched, and clinical text, and 3) public models and code to advance multilingual clinical NLP, directly addressing the variability of LLMs in extracting information across languages.
proposals.novelty: Unlike prior work on multilingual sentence embeddings (e.g., LASER, LaBSE) which rely on large parallel corpora, our method uses a descriptor-based multi-task setup to explicitly decouple semantic representation from language-specific syntax, aiming for true zero-shot transfer on STS without direct cross-lingual supervision.

proposals.title: Multi-Agent Labeling Systems for Scalable, Interpretable Semantic Textual Similarity Dataset Generation
proposals.motivation: Interpretable Semantic Textual Similarity (iSTS) datasets, which provide fine-grained alignment and relation labels between text spans, are crucial for advancing explainable NLP in clinical and other high-stakes domains. However, their creation is prohibitively expensive and slow, relying on expert annotation. This bottleneck limits the scale and diversity of iSTS resources, hindering the development of robust, interpretable models for real-world applications.
proposals.hypothesis: A multi-agent system (MAS) framework, where specialized LLM-based agents emulate distinct expert annotator roles and debate rationales, can generate high-quality, large-scale iSTS datasets at a fraction of the cost and time of traditional expert annotation.
proposals.method: We will design a multi-agent system with three specialized LLM agents (e.g., based on GPT-4 or Llama 2): a Decomposition Agent to identify candidate spans, an Alignment Agent to propose relations, and a Critique Agent to validate labels. Inspired by Mihalcea's work on agent-based simulation and 'in-the-wild' evaluation, the system will incorporate a debate mechanism where agents must justify their decisions using natural language descriptors. We will use a high-quality, small-scale expert-labeled iSTS clinical corpus (e.g., from bladder cancer notes) as a seed for few-shot prompting and iterative refinement. The final labels will be determined via a learned aggregator that weights agent votes based on their historical performance on held-out seed data.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver: 1) a novel, open-source framework for scalable iSTS dataset creation, 2) a new large-scale, publicly available iSTS dataset focused on clinical narratives, and 3) an analysis of the 'agent paradox' in this setting—evaluating whether multi-agent debate improves label quality and rationale consistency over a single LLM annotator.
proposals.novelty: While LLMs have been used for data augmentation, applying a deliberative multi-agent architecture specifically for the complex, structured task of iSTS annotation—and rigorously evaluating its ability to produce human-interpretable rationales—is a novel approach that moves beyond simple one-shot labeling.

proposals.title: Interpretable Semantic Textual Similarity for Clinical QA System Evaluation and Enhancement
proposals.motivation: Current LLM-based clinical question-answering (QA) systems are powerful but opaque, making it difficult for clinicians to trust their outputs. Standard STS metrics provide a single similarity score, offering no insight into *why* a system's answer is deemed correct or incorrect, which is critical for safety-critical medical applications. An interpretable STS framework could bridge this gap, providing actionable feedback for system improvement and user trust.
proposals.hypothesis: Integrating interpretable STS models that generate fine-grained, aspect-based similarity rationales (e.g., semantic role alignment, negation handling) into the evaluation and feedback loop of a clinical QA system will improve the system's performance and provide clinicians with transparent, justifiable confidence scores for generated answers.
proposals.method: We will adapt pre-2026 interpretable STS architectures, such as decomposable attention models or rationale-generating Transformers, to produce similarity scores alongside token- or phrase-level alignment maps. These models will be fine-tuned on a **multi**-aspect, clinically **labeled** dataset (e.g., derived from clinical entailment tasks). The interpretable STS output will be integrated into a QA **systems** pipeline in two ways: 1) as a refined evaluation metric beyond BLEU/ROUGE for our **Evaluation Framework for AI Systems in 'the Wild'**, and 2) as a feedback signal to iteratively refine answer generation through a **user**-in-the-loop simulation where the rationale highlights errors.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a novel, interpretability-enhanced evaluation framework for clinical NLP systems, moving beyond black-box metrics. It will demonstrate how interpretable STS can be practically **integrated** into downstream applications to improve system **performance** and provide actionable **descriptors** of model behavior, directly addressing the transparency needs in **clinical** settings highlighted by Mihalcea's prior work on LLM variability in medical records.
proposals.novelty: While interpretable STS models exist, their application as an integrated component for both evaluation *and* iterative improvement of a domain-specific (clinical) QA system is novel. This moves interpretability from a post-hoc analysis tool to a core, functional element of the AI **systems** development loop.

proposals.title: A Hybrid Multi-Stage Framework for High-Quality, Scalable Semantic Textual Similarity Annotation in Clinical Domains
proposals.motivation: Creating high-quality labeled datasets for Semantic Textual Similarity (STS) in clinical NLP is bottlenecked by the cost and scarcity of expert annotators, while crowdsourcing alone often yields unreliable labels for complex medical text. This limits the development and robust evaluation of LLMs and AI systems for critical applications like information extraction from medical records. A scalable, cost-effective annotation methodology that ensures expert-level quality is urgently needed.
proposals.hypothesis: A multi-stage annotation framework that strategically combines crowdsourced labels with targeted expert adjudication and quality control can produce STS datasets with accuracy comparable to full-expert annotation at a significantly lower cost and higher scale.
proposals.method: We propose a hybrid, iterative framework: 1) Use a multi-armed bandit algorithm to dynamically route text pairs to crowdworkers or experts based on predicted ambiguity and worker performance. 2) Implement a consensus model with disagreement detection to flag low-confidence crowd labels for expert review. 3) Employ a small set of domain experts in a final validation loop, using their adjudicated labels to fine-tune a BERT-based quality filter for continuous improvement of the crowd task allocation. The system will be evaluated on a bladder cancer clinical note corpus.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a novel, generalizable annotation pipeline for creating high-fidelity clinical STS datasets efficiently. It will also produce a new public benchmark dataset for clinical STS and insights into the cost-quality trade-offs in hybrid annotation, directly informing future evaluations of AI systems 'in the wild'.
