proposals.novelty: Unlike prior work that statically combines crowd and expert input, our framework uses active learning and bandit algorithms to dynamically optimize the annotation workflow, making the integration of scale and quality an adaptive, learned process rather than a fixed protocol.

proposals.title: GraphMatchSTS: A Novel Framework for Semantic Textual Similarity via Graph Matching and Multi-Agent Refinement
proposals.motivation: Current Semantic Textual Similarity (STS) methods, especially those based on sentence embeddings from LLMs, can struggle with fine-grained, interpretable alignment of semantic structures and relational meaning. This is critical in clinical and performance domains where precise matching of concepts, modifiers, and relations (e.g., in medical records) is essential for reliable information extraction and evaluation. A graph-based approach offers a structured, descriptor-rich representation that could capture this complexity more transparently than dense vector models.
proposals.hypothesis: Representing sentences as semantic graphs (encoding entities, actions, and their dependencies) and applying advanced graph matching algorithms will yield more accurate, interpretable, and robust similarity scores for STS, particularly in specialized domains like clinical text, compared to state-of-the-art embedding-based methods.
proposals.method: We will model sentences as directed graphs using pre-2026 NLP toolkits (e.g., spaCy, Stanford CoreNLP) to extract nodes (content words/phrases) and edges (syntactic dependencies, semantic roles). For graph matching, we will adapt and evaluate specific algorithms like the Weisfeiler-Lehman kernel for similarity scoring and the Graduated Assignment algorithm for node alignment. This core system will be integrated into a multi-agent evaluation framework, where different agents (specializing in syntactic, semantic, or lexical matching) propose and critique alignments, refining the final similarity judgment.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute: 1) a novel, interpretable graph-based paradigm for STS that provides explicit alignment paths between sentences, 2) a publicly released benchmark for graph-based STS, particularly for clinical language, and 3) insights into the integration of symbolic graph matching with multi-agent systems for complex NLP evaluation tasks.
proposals.novelty: While graph representations exist in NLP, their direct application to STS via formal graph matching algorithms is underexplored. The novelty lies in the systematic transfer of these algorithms from computer vision and pattern recognition to STS, coupled with a multi-agent refinement process inspired by the complexity of human judgment, moving beyond simple cosine similarity of embeddings.

proposals.title: Interpretable Cross-Modal Semantic Similarity: A Framework for Aligning and Explaining Text-Image Pairs
proposals.motivation: Semantic Textual Similarity (STS) provides interpretable measures of meaning alignment between text pairs, but modern AI systems increasingly process multi-modal data (e.g., clinical notes and medical images). Existing cross-modal retrieval models (e.g., CLIP) produce similarity scores but lack the fine-grained, human-interpretable descriptors that explain *why* a text and an image are semantically aligned, which is critical for trustworthy applications in clinical and real-world evaluation settings.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that by decomposing cross-modal similarity into a set of discrete, interpretable semantic dimensions (e.g., objects, actions, attributes, relations), we can generate human-readable explanations for text-image alignment that are both faithful to the model's reasoning and useful for end-user validation.
proposals.method: We will extend interpretable STS frameworks to the text-image domain. First, we will create a new labeled dataset of text-image pairs annotated with fine-grained similarity dimensions, building on Mihalcea's work on clinical descriptors and variability in LLM extraction. We will then develop a multi-agent system where specialized agents (e.g., for object detection, relation extraction, attribute classification) propose alignment scores and rationales for specific dimensions. A final arbitrator agent, using a transformer-based architecture (e.g., a modified multi-modal BERT or a late-fusion of CLIP embeddings with agent outputs), will synthesize these into a final similarity score and a unified natural language explanation.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute 1) a novel framework for interpretable cross-modal similarity, 2) a new benchmark dataset with fine-grained, multi-modal similarity annotations, and 3) an evaluation protocol for 'explainability in the wild,' assessing how well generated explanations aid users in validating AI system outputs in domains like clinical data analysis.
proposals.novelty: While multi-modal alignment and interpretable NLP are active areas, the explicit fusion of interpretable STS techniques with a multi-agent architecture to produce decomposable, descriptor-based explanations for text-image similarity is a novel and non-obvious synthesis, moving beyond black-box similarity scores.

proposals.title: Adversarial Stress-Testing for Semantic Textual Similarity: A Multi-Agent Framework for Generating and Evaluating Challenging Clinical Datasets
proposals.motivation: Semantic Textual Similarity (STS) models are crucial for clinical NLP tasks like information extraction from patient records, but their robustness is often overestimated on standard benchmarks. Pre-2026, there is a lack of systematic adversarial datasets that probe specific failure modes in STS, such as sensitivity to negation, temporal shifts, or domain-specific paraphrasing, which limits our understanding of model reliability in high-stakes medical applications.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a multi-agent generation framework can systematically create adversarial STS sentence pairs that expose significant, interpretable weaknesses in state-of-the-art STS models, particularly in the clinical domain, and that these weaknesses correlate with poor performance on real-world extraction tasks.
proposals.method: We will develop a multi-agent system where specialized agents (e.g., for negation, lexical substitution, temporal reasoning) use instruction-tuned LLMs (e.g., GPT-4, Llama 2) to perturb sentences from clinical corpora (e.g., MIMIC-III, bladder cancer notes) along defined linguistic dimensions. A separate 'validator' agent, guided by human-crafted rules and entailment models, will filter generations to ensure they preserve gold-standard similarity labels. We will evaluate the resulting dataset on diverse STS models (sentence-transformers, clinical BERT) and analyze failure patterns using the evaluation framework from Mihalcea's 'in the wild' research.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute 1) a novel, publicly available adversarial STS benchmark focused on clinical language, 2) a reproducible, multi-agent framework for adversarial data generation, and 3) a detailed analysis of STS model failure modes, providing actionable insights for improving robustness and interpretability in medical NLP systems.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in applying a controllable, multi-agent generation paradigm—inspired by the 'Agent Paradox' work—to create structured adversarial challenges for STS, moving beyond simple noise injection or existing adversarial NLP templates to target clinically-relevant semantic phenomena.

proposals.title: Latent Clinical Descriptors: Enhancing Knowledge-Based Similarity with Latent Semantic Analysis for Patient Record Comparison
proposals.motivation: Current knowledge-based similarity frameworks for clinical text, such as those using SNOMED-CT or UMLS concept descriptors, rely on explicit semantic networks, which can miss nuanced thematic relationships between patient narratives. This limits their performance in real-world applications like cohort identification or outcome prediction from unstructured records. Integrating latent, data-driven topic structures could bridge this gap, capturing implicit clinical patterns not codified in static ontologies.
proposals.hypothesis: Augmenting a structured knowledge-based similarity framework with latent topics derived via LSA will yield a more robust and clinically meaningful representation of patient records, leading to superior performance in downstream tasks compared to purely knowledge-based or purely latent models.
proposals.method: We will develop a hybrid framework that processes clinical notes (e.g., from bladder cancer records) to extract UMLS concepts as explicit descriptors. Concurrently, we will apply LSA (via Truncated SVD on a TF-IDF matrix) to the same corpus to derive latent topic distributions. The final patient representation will be a fused vector of explicit concept embeddings and latent topic weights. Similarity will be computed using a weighted combination of cosine similarity in both spaces. We will evaluate using labeled tasks such as patient phenotype matching and outcome prediction, benchmarking against standalone knowledge-based and topic models.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel, hybrid similarity model for clinical NLP that combines the interpretability of knowledge bases with the contextual flexibility of latent semantics. It will provide a new evaluation framework for AI systems 'in the wild,' assessing how such fusion improves robustness to linguistic variability in real clinical text. The resulting descriptors could enhance multi-agent systems for healthcare by providing richer patient representations for decision support.
proposals.novelty: While LSA and knowledge graphs are well-established separately, their synergistic integration for clinical similarity is unexplored. The novelty lies in formally combining explicit medical ontology concepts with implicit LSA-derived topics within a unified similarity framework, moving beyond simple ensemble methods to a principled fusion.

proposals.title: Evaluating and Enhancing LLM-Based Similarity for Very Short Text: A Focus on Clinical Queries and Search Snippets
proposals.motivation: Measuring semantic similarity for very short texts, such as clinical search queries or user-generated descriptors, is critical for robust information retrieval and AI-assisted decision systems in healthcare. Pre-2026 LLM-based similarity metrics, while powerful for longer passages, often fail on short snippets due to sparse contextual signals and high sensitivity to lexical variation. This gap hinders the deployment of reliable AI systems in the wild, particularly in clinical domains where precision is paramount.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a multi-agent evaluation framework, combined with task-specific fine-tuning on a labeled corpus of short clinical text pairs, can significantly improve the robustness and accuracy of LLM-based similarity measures for very short texts compared to standard off-the-shelf embeddings.
proposals.method: We will create a novel, high-quality labeled dataset of very short clinical query pairs (e.g., 'bladder cancer recurrence' vs. 'BCG failure') with similarity scores annotated by clinical experts. Using this data, we will fine-tune a contrastive learning objective with a Sentence-BERT architecture on a clinical corpus. The core evaluation will employ a multi-agent system where specialized 'critic' agents (e.g., a lexical expert, a clinical concept agent) analyze LLM similarity outputs against human judgments, identifying failure modes and variability as in Mihalcea's prior work on evaluation frameworks.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute: 1) a benchmark dataset and a multi-agent evaluation protocol for short-text similarity in the clinical domain, and 2) a fine-tuned model that provides more reliable and interpretable similarity scores for search queries and snippets. This directly advances the development of evaluable and trustworthy AI systems for real-world clinical information retrieval.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the targeted adaptation and rigorous evaluation of LLM similarity for the understudied domain of very short clinical texts, moving beyond document-level analysis. The integration of a multi-agent diagnostic framework for model evaluation in this specific context is a novel methodological contribution.

proposals.title: Kernel-Based Multi-Document Clustering for Clinical Cohort Discovery from Unstructured Medical Narratives
proposals.motivation: The proliferation of unstructured clinical notes in electronic health records presents a significant challenge for cohort discovery and retrospective research. Manually reviewing thousands of documents is infeasible, and existing keyword-based or simple vector similarity methods often fail to capture the nuanced semantic relationships between patient narratives, limiting the ability to identify clinically relevant patient subgroups.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a kernel-based clustering approach, using a pairwise semantic similarity function derived from a domain-tuned language model as the kernel, will produce more clinically coherent and meaningful document clusters from a corpus of unstructured medical records than standard clustering methods applied to generic embeddings.
proposals.method: We will leverage a corpus of de-identified bladder cancer patient notes. Pairwise semantic similarity will be computed using a clinical-domain BERT model (e.g., BioBERT or ClinicalBERT) fine-tuned for semantic textual similarity on medical data. This similarity matrix will serve as a kernel for Spectral Clustering, chosen for its ability to leverage pairwise affinities directly. We will compare this to baseline methods (K-Means, HDBSCAN) applied to standard sentence-BERT embeddings. Evaluation will use both intrinsic metrics (silhouette score) and, crucially, extrinsic validation via clinical expert assessment of cluster coherence using pre-defined clinical descriptors.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a robust, semantics-driven framework for unsupervised discovery of patient cohorts from narrative text, directly applicable to clinical research. It will provide empirical evidence on the effectiveness of kernel methods with LLM-derived similarities for real-world medical data, extending Mihalcea's prior work on LLM variability in clinical information extraction towards a synthesis task.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the integration of a domain-specific semantic similarity kernel with spectral clustering for the clinical document organization task, moving beyond generic embedding spaces to a similarity function explicitly tailored for medical narrative semantics and evaluated in a real-world, user-in-the-loop validation framework.

proposals.title: Affective Emergence: A Multi-Channel, Attention-Based Framework for Modeling Emotion in Language
proposals.motivation: Current NLP models for affective computing often treat emotion as a static label derived from a single linguistic level, such as lexicon or syntax, failing to capture the dynamic, context-dependent nature of emotional meaning. This limits their robustness and psychological plausibility, especially in high-stakes domains like clinical NLP, where pragmatic framing can critically alter interpretation. A framework that models how affective meaning emerges from the interaction of multiple linguistic channels is needed to advance both theory and application.
proposals.hypothesis: Affective meaning in language is an emergent property of the dynamic, context-modulated interaction between lexical, syntactic, and pragmatic information channels, rather than a fixed property of any single channel.
proposals.method: We will develop a multi-channel neural architecture that processes lexical (e.g., valence-arousal embeddings), syntactic (dependency parse features), and pragmatic (e.g., dialogue act, politeness markers) features in parallel. A multi-head attention mechanism will model their interactions and dynamically weight their contributions based on the broader discourse context. The system will be trained and evaluated on existing multi-party, affectively labeled datasets (e.g., IEMOCAP, clinical counseling transcripts) and stress-tested using adversarial examples where channels conflict.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel computational framework for modeling emergent affective meaning, advancing theoretical understanding in computational linguistics. It will yield a more robust and interpretable system for emotion recognition, with direct applications in clinical NLP for analyzing patient-provider interactions and mental health support systems.
proposals.novelty: Unlike prior work that fuses features statically, this framework explicitly models the dynamic, competitive interaction between linguistic channels using attention, treating affective interpretation as a context-driven emergent process rather than a simple aggregation.

proposals.title: Controlled Affective Summarization: A Syntactic-Pragmatic Framework for Preserving Stance and Certainty in Clinical Text Abstracts
proposals.motivation: Current abstractive summarization models, including LLMs, prioritize factual fidelity but often fail to preserve the author's nuanced affective stance, such as certainty, doubt, or argumentative tone, which are critical for decision-making in domains like clinical note review. These non-lexical cues are encoded in syntax (e.g., modality, mood) and pragmatics (e.g., focus, discourse relations). An evaluation framework for AI systems 'in the wild' must account for this higher-level semantic preservation to ensure summaries are not just factually correct but pragmatically faithful.
proposals.hypothesis: By explicitly modeling and controlling for syntactic-pragmatic features that convey affective meaning, an abstractive summarization system can generate more pragmatically faithful summaries that preserve the source text's stance and certainty, outperforming content-only models on measures of pragmatic and affective preservation.
proposals.method: We propose a two-stage, multi-system pipeline. First, an extractor ranks sentences using a joint salience score combining BERT-based informational relevance and a syntactic-pragmatic feature vector (extracting modality, negation, sentence type, and discourse connectors via spaCy and a pre-2026 parser like Stanford CoreNLP). This ranked list and its feature profiles form a control scaffold. Second, a controlled abstractive generator (e.g., a fine-tuned T5 or BART model) uses these features as control codes during sequence generation, trained on a labeled dataset where target summaries are annotated for affective features. The system will be evaluated on a corpus of clinical narratives (e.g., bladder cancer notes) using both ROUGE and novel pragmatic fidelity metrics.
proposals.contribution: This work contributes 1) a novel summarization framework that integrates explicit syntactic-pragmatic control, 2) a methodology for creating labeled data for affective feature alignment in summarization, and 3) new evaluation metrics for pragmatic faithfulness, extending prior work on clinical information extraction and evaluation frameworks for real-world AI systems.
proposals.novelty: The approach moves beyond lexical sentiment to model affect via grammatical and discourse structures, and it uniquely uses these features as direct constraints for generation, a fusion not explored in pre-2026 summarization or controlled text generation work.

proposals.title: Modeling the Manifold of Plausible Emotions: A Disagreement-Aware Framework for Subjective NLP
proposals.motivation: Current emotion recognition systems treat high-agreement labels as ground truth, forcing a reductive consensus that erases the inherent subjectivity and cultural nuance of emotional expression. This is particularly problematic for clinical and real-world AI systems, where understanding minority perspectives and context-dependence is crucial for performance and fairness. Annotator disagreement, often treated as noise, is instead a rich signal of the multiplicity of valid interpretations.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that annotator disagreement in emotion labeling can be computationally decomposed into interpretable latent factors (e.g., cultural perspective, lexical ambiguity), and that modeling these factors will allow us to represent emotional truth as a probability distribution or manifold, leading to AI systems that better capture nuance and variability.
proposals.method: We will develop a multi-encoder neural architecture where a shared encoder processes the text, and multiple specialized 'perspective' heads, informed by annotator metadata (if available), learn to capture distinct labeling rationales. Using techniques from multi-task learning and variational inference (e.g., a Conditional Variational Autoencoder framework), we will model the distribution over plausible labels for a given stimulus. We will validate on existing multi-annotator datasets (e.g., GoEmotions) and a new labeled dataset of clinical narratives, evaluating both the quality of the decomposed factors and the system's ability to predict the full distribution of human responses.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel computational framework for treating subjectivity in NLP, moving beyond single-point estimates to model a manifold of plausible truths. It will contribute new evaluation metrics for distributional prediction in subjective tasks and offer insights into the sources of human disagreement in emotion interpretation, with direct implications for building more robust and nuanced clinical and user-facing AI systems.
proposals.novelty: While prior work has modeled annotator bias or aggregated labels probabilistically, our approach uniquely seeks to *decompose* disagreement into interpretable, disentangled latent factors that correspond to plausible human perspectives, formally reconceptualizing the learning objective for subjective tasks.

proposals.title: Consensus as Ground Truth: A Multi-Annotator Framework for Micro-Summarization of Subjective Language
proposals.motivation: Current summarization models, including LLMs, struggle with short, subjective texts like social media posts or patient narratives, where the core content is an emotional stance rather than factual propositions. These models often produce a single, potentially biased summary, ignoring the inherent interpretative variability that human annotators exhibit. This limits their reliability in high-stakes, user-facing applications like clinical note analysis or social media monitoring, where transparency about contested interpretations is crucial.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that modeling the distribution of plausible human summaries for subjective content, and training a model to generate a core summary weighted by annotator agreement, will produce more faithful and transparent micro-summaries than models trained on a single ground truth.
proposals.method: We will collect a new dataset of short, subjective texts (e.g., patient forum posts, tweets) with multiple human-written micro-summaries per instance. Using pre-2026 transformer-based architectures (e.g., T5, BART), we will train a model with a dual objective: 1) to generate a consensus summary, where the loss function weights phrases by their frequency across annotators, and 2) to output a per-element confidence score derived from the annotator agreement distribution. We will adapt techniques from uncertainty quantification (e.g., Bayesian deep learning or ensemble methods) to estimate this confidence.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel evaluation framework for subjective summarization that redefines ground truth as a distribution. It will also deliver a practical model that produces compact, interpretable summaries with explicit confidence signals, advancing the field towards more transparent and trustworthy NLP systems for real-world, user-centered applications.
proposals.novelty: Unlike prior work on multi-document or abstractive summarization, this method explicitly models and exposes interpretative disagreement as a core feature of the output, moving beyond seeking a single 'correct' summary for inherently subjective language.

proposals.title: Modeling the Emotional Niche: A Genre-Adaptive Framework for Robust Emotion Classification in Diverse Textual Ecosystems
proposals.motivation: Current emotion classification systems, often trained on constrained datasets like news headlines, fail to generalize to diverse textual genres such as blogs, social narratives, and literary fiction. This limits their utility in real-world applications like mental health monitoring, narrative analysis, and user modeling. A method is needed to explicitly account for genre-specific linguistic and contextual features to achieve robust cross-genre performance.
proposals.hypothesis: By explicitly modeling the relationship between emotional states and genre-specific textual features—treating genres as distinct ecosystems—a classification system can be adapted to new genres more effectively than through direct transfer or simple fine-tuning of a monolithic model.
proposals.method: We will develop a pipeline inspired by Ecological Niche Modeling. First, we will train a base emotion classifier (e.g., a BERT-based model) on a multi-genre, labeled corpus, extracting a rich set of linguistic, stylistic, and contextual descriptors. We will then train a meta-model (e.g., a multi-task or conditional transformer architecture) that uses these descriptors to predict genre-conditioned emotion distributions. For adaptation to a new, unlabeled genre, we will use unsupervised domain adaptation techniques (e.g., adversarial training or self-training) to calibrate the model to the new genre's feature distribution, effectively mapping its 'emotional niche.'
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel, principled framework for cross-genre emotion classification that moves beyond simple domain adaptation. It will yield a publicly available, multi-genre benchmark dataset with fine-grained descriptors and a flexible model that can be conditioned on genre-specific features for improved performance in the wild.
proposals.novelty: The core novelty is the conceptual and methodological adaptation of Ecological Niche Modeling to NLP, explicitly framing genres as ecosystems and emotional expressions as species with niche constraints. This provides a more interpretable and structured approach to domain shift than standard domain-adversarial methods.

proposals.title: Conflict-Aware Bayesian Fusion for Robust Emotion Lexicon Integration
proposals.motivation: Emotion lexicons are foundational for affective computing and clinical NLP, but resources like NRC-EmoLex, EmoSenticNet, and DepecheMood vary in granularity, scales, and labels, creating noise and conflict when naïvely combined. This undermines the reliability of downstream systems for sentiment analysis, mental health assessment, and human-agent interaction. A principled, data-driven integration framework is needed to produce a unified, high-coverage lexicon that resolves these inconsistencies.
proposals.hypothesis: A Bayesian fusion mechanism that dynamically weights lexicon contributions based on contextual word embeddings and annotated gold-standard data will probabilistically resolve inter-lexicon conflicts and produce a unified resource that outperforms any single source or simple union in emotion classification tasks.
proposals.method: We will first build a context-sensitive alignment model using transformer-based embeddings (e.g., BERT, RoBERTa) to map disparate emotion categories and intensity scores onto a unified psychological space (e.g., valence-arousal-dominance). The core is a probabilistic graphical model performing Bayesian fusion, where each lexicon is a noisy observer with a reliability prior. These priors will be updated via expectation-maximization using labeled data from existing corpora (e.g., EmoBank, GoEmotions), dynamically weighting lexicon entries based on agreement with context and annotations.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a novel, publicly available unified emotion lexicon with probabilistic confidence scores. It will provide a generalizable framework for multi-resource integration in NLP, directly improving the performance and interpretability of emotion classification systems in clinical and social computing applications.
proposals.novelty: Unlike prior union/intersection approaches, this method explicitly models and resolves conflicts via a dynamic, context-aware Bayesian model, treating lexicon integration as a probabilistic inference problem rather than a static merge.

proposals.title: EmoMeta: A Super-Ensemble Framework for Robust Emotion Classification by Integrating Diverse Lexical Resources
proposals.motivation: Emotion classification is crucial for clinical NLP applications, such as analyzing patient narratives, but remains challenging due to lexical ambiguity and context-dependence. While numerous lexical resources (e.g., NRC, SentiWordNet) exist, each has inherent biases and coverage gaps, leading to inconsistent and unreliable predictions when used in isolation. A principled method to combine these heterogeneous 'weak' resources into a robust, high-accuracy system is needed for reliable real-world deployment.
proposals.hypothesis: A machine-learned meta-model, trained to understand the context-specific predictive reliability of multiple lexical semantic resources, can dynamically combine their outputs to achieve higher emotion classification accuracy than any single resource or a simple, static fusion method.
proposals.method: We will treat resources (WordNet-Affect, SentiWordNet, NRC, EmoLex, SenticNet) as base models, extracting their emotion scores for text units. A feature vector for each unit will encode scores from all resources, lexical descriptors (e.g., POS, concreteness), and contextual features. Using labeled emotion corpora (e.g., EmoBank, ISEAR), we will train a meta-model (e.g., a multi-layer perceptron or gradient-boosted trees) to predict gold-standard labels, learning optimal resource weighting. The final system will apply this trained meta-model to new text for ensemble prediction.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a novel, high-accuracy emotion classification system validated on clinical and social media corpora. It will provide a generalizable framework for integrating heterogeneous lexical knowledge sources in NLP. The analysis will yield insights into the systematic strengths and biases of major emotion lexicons, guiding future resource development.
proposals.novelty: This adapts the super-ensemble concept from climate modeling to lexical resource fusion, moving beyond simple voting or averaging. The meta-model learns dynamic, context-aware weighting, a significant advance over static, heuristic combination methods prevalent in pre-2026 sentiment and emotion analysis.

proposals.title: Uncertainty-Aware Ordinal Regression for Emotion Intensity and Valence: A Multi-Task Framework
proposals.motivation: Accurate modeling of emotion intensity and valence is critical for robust affective computing systems in clinical, educational, and human-computer interaction applications. However, existing regression or classification approaches often ignore the inherent ordinal nature of these dimensions and fail to quantify prediction uncertainty, which is essential given the subjectivity of emotional expression and annotation. This limits the reliability and interpretability of these systems in real-world, high-stakes scenarios.
proposals.hypothesis: Modeling emotion intensity and valence as a multi-task ordinal regression problem with explicit uncertainty estimation will yield more accurate, interpretable, and reliable predictions than standard regression or classification methods, particularly for ambiguous or subjective cases.
proposals.method: We will adapt the CORAL (Conditional Ordinal Regression for neural networks) framework for a multi-task setting, using a shared encoder (e.g., a pre-trained transformer like BERT or RoBERTa) with separate ordinal output layers for intensity and valence. The model will output a probability distribution across ordered bins (e.g., 1-7 Likert scales) for each dimension. We will incorporate uncertainty estimation via Monte Carlo Dropout at inference time to quantify epistemic uncertainty. The framework will be evaluated on established, labeled datasets (e.g., EmoBank, GoEmotions) and validated in a user study simulating a clinical or performance context.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel, principled framework for ordinal affective prediction with built-in confidence measures, directly addressing the 'Agent Paradox' challenge of reliable AI behavior. It will contribute a new evaluation metric for uncertainty in affective systems 'in the wild' and offer a methodologically sound approach for extracting nuanced, structured descriptors from language, applicable to clinical NLP tasks like symptom severity scoring.
proposals.novelty: While ordinal regression is established in medical imaging, its application to affective computing with integrated uncertainty quantification is novel. The multi-task adaptation for concurrent intensity and valence prediction, drawing an explicit analogy to clinical severity staging, represents a significant methodological advance over treating these as independent scalar regressions.

proposals.title: Affective Seismographs: Transferring Visual Pattern Recognition Architectures for Emotion Detection in Text
proposals.motivation: Accurately recognizing nuanced and dynamic emotional states in text is critical for clinical applications (e.g., mental health monitoring) and human-AI interaction. Current methods, including LLMs, often treat emotion as a static label, failing to capture the temporal contours and multi-scale affective transitions inherent in language. A novel methodological framework is needed to model text as a continuous affective signal, moving beyond bag-of-words or sequence-level classification.
proposals.hypothesis: By reinterpreting textual sequences as one-dimensional affective signals (via contextual embeddings or syntactic dependency graphs) and systematically adapting convolutional neural network (CNN) architectures and attention mechanisms from visual affect recognition, we can develop models that more accurately detect local emotional cues and their hierarchical composition in language.
proposals.method: We will treat sequences of word/sentence embeddings (e.g., from BERT) or nodes in syntactic graphs as 1D time-series 'images'. We will adapt 2D CNN architectures (e.g., ResNet, VGG) for 1D convolution to detect local affective 'textons' and build hierarchical representations. Spatial attention mechanisms (e.g., SE-Net, CBAM) will be reinterpreted as 'temporal attention' to weight emotionally salient segments. The framework will be evaluated on multi-label, emotion-intensity datasets (e.g., GoEmotions, EmoInt) and clinical narratives, benchmarking against standard sequence models (LSTMs, Transformers).
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a new paradigm for textual affect recognition, introducing architectures that explicitly model emotional contours and transitions. It will provide a systematic methodology for cross-domain transfer of visual pattern recognition techniques to NLP. The resulting models will offer interpretable, granular descriptors of emotional content, advancing applications in clinical informatics and user-centered AI systems.
proposals.novelty: The core novelty is the structured, seismology-inspired analogy: directly transferring the methodological framework of visual affect recognition (local feature detection, hierarchical CNNs, spatial attention) to text, treating it as a 1D signal, rather than merely applying existing NLP models. This cross-disciplinary transfer is non-obvious and distinct from standard sequential or transformer-based approaches.

proposals.title: Affective Consensus Grounding: Cross-Modal Supervision for Noise-Robust Emotion Recognition
proposals.motivation: Current emotion recognition models, especially for text, suffer from high label subjectivity and annotator bias, limiting their reliability in clinical or real-world performance settings. Cross-modal datasets (e.g., images with captions, news events with articles) offer parallel affective signals, but prior fusion methods simply concatenate features, propagating noise. A method to distill a more stable, universally interpretable emotional representation is needed for robust AI systems.
proposals.hypothesis: The high-agreement emotional consensus derived from annotations in one modality (e.g., visual) can serve as a superior supervisory signal for training a model in another modality (e.g., textual), leading to more grounded and noise-robust affective representations than learning from raw, noisy labels directly.
proposals.method: We will curate or utilize existing multi-modal datasets (e.g., from social media or news) where the same stimulus has independent text and image labels. A consensus module will compute high-certainty emotion labels from the visual annotations using inter-annotator agreement metrics. This consensus will then supervise a transformer-based text encoder (e.g., BERT or RoBERTa) via a cross-modal distillation loss, such as KL-divergence, forcing the text model's predictions to align with the visually-grounded consensus. A parallel, symmetrically trained visual model will use textual consensus for supervision.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel cross-modal learning paradigm that improves the stability and generalizability of textual emotion recognition. It will also provide a new framework for evaluating AI systems 'in the wild' by measuring cross-modal consensus as a proxy for grounding, advancing beyond single-modality benchmarks.
proposals.novelty: Unlike standard multi-modal fusion, the core novelty is using one modality not as a complementary feature but as a source of high-certainty supervisory signal for another, explicitly filtering label noise to discover a cross-modally grounded affective representation.

proposals.title: Cross-Modal Transfer of Affective Descriptors: Adapting Visual Affect Recognition Architectures for Clinical Text Analysis
proposals.motivation: Affective computing in text, crucial for clinical NLP and user interaction analysis, often relies on lexical models or LLM fine-tuning, which can be brittle and data-hungry. In contrast, visual affect recognition has developed robust, descriptor-based models (e.g., Action Units, valence-arousal-dominance) that capture nuanced, compositional emotional states. Transferring these structured, multi-dimensional frameworks to language could provide a more systematic and interpretable basis for analyzing emotional content in clinical narratives and user-generated text.
proposals.hypothesis: Architectural and representational principles from state-of-the-art visual affect recognition models (e.g., CNN-based feature extractors for emotional descriptors) can be effectively adapted to the textual domain, leading to more accurate and generalizable models for detecting subtle, multi-faceted emotions in text compared to standard text-only approaches.
proposals.method: We will adapt the multi-task, descriptor-based learning paradigm from visual models like AffectNet-trained CNNs. Using a transformer (e.g., BERT, ClinicalBERT) as a base feature extractor for text, we will train parallel output heads to predict a structured set of affective descriptors (e.g., Ekman's basic emotions, sentiment polarity, intensity) derived from pre-2026 textual emotion corpora. This will be evaluated on labeled clinical datasets (e.g., from the referenced bladder cancer study) and social media data, comparing performance against monolithic LLM fine-tuning and traditional lexicon methods.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel, descriptor-driven architecture for textual emotion recognition that improves robustness and interpretability. It will provide a framework for analyzing the 'performance' of emotional expression in language, with direct applications in clinical NLP for extracting patient states and in evaluating multi-agent system interactions.
proposals.novelty: While cross-modal transfer is common, directly translating the specific architectural paradigm of visual affect descriptors (treating emotion as a set of composable, measurable components) to text is unexplored. This moves beyond simple sentiment to a more human-cognition-inspired model of affective language.

ideas.title: Explore more sophisticated linguistic features
ideas.description: Investigate deeper semantic and pragmatic features beyond the lexical and syntactic ones used.
ideas.sourceTitle: The Lie Detector: Explorations in the Automatic Recognition of Deceptive Language

ideas.title: Cross-modal consensus learning for affective grounding
ideas.description: This research direction proposes a novel cross-modal learning paradigm for emotion recognition. Instead of adapting visual models directly to text, the method uses datasets where the same affective stimuli (e.g., news events, social media posts) have both visual and textual representations, each with independent annotator labels. The core innovation is to train a model where the learning signal for text is not its own raw labels, but the high-agreement emotional consensus derived from the corresponding visual annotations (or vice-versa). This forces the model to learn textual affective features that align with the more grounded, high-certainty interpretations from another modality, thereby filtering out noise and subjective bias to discover a more universally interpretable and stable emotional representation across media.

ideas.title: Ensemble Lexical Resource Integration via a Meta-Model for Emotion Classification
ideas.description: This idea proposes to improve emotion classification accuracy by integrating multiple lexical semantic resources through an ensemble meta-model approach, analogous to multi-model ensembles in climate science. In weather prediction, a super-ensemble learns to weight the outputs of various global circulation models to produce a superior forecast. Adapted to this context, diverse resources like WordNet-Affect, SentiWordNet, NRC Emotion Lexicon, EmoLex, and SenticNet would be treated as individual 'models'. A machine learning meta-model would be trained on gold-standard emotion-annotated corpora to learn the predictive reliability and biases of each resource for different words, phrases, or contexts. For any new text, the meta-model would dynamically combine the scores from all resources, leveraging their collective strengths and mitigating individual weaknesses, thereby achieving higher classification accuracy than any single resource or a simple fusion method.

ideas.title: Challenge language-agnostic semantic representation
ideas.description: Explore if a truly language-agnostic semantic representation is possible or if language-specific nuances are fundamental.
ideas.sourceTitle: SemEval-2016 Task 1: Semantic Textual Similarity, Monolingual and Cross-Lingual Evaluation

ideas.title: Create the inverse problem: text expansion
ideas.description: Use the graph to identify and add relevant explanatory sentences to a short seed text.
ideas.sourceTitle: Graph-based Ranking Algorithms for Sentence Extraction, Applied to Text Summarization

ideas.title: Create adversarial STS datasets
ideas.description: Systematically generate challenging sentence pairs to stress-test STS model robustness and interpretability.
ideas.sourceTitle: SemEval-2015 Task 2: Semantic Textual Similarity, English, Spanish and Pilot on Interpretability

ideas.title: Adapting Ecological Niche Modeling to Map Emotional Expression Across Textual Genres
ideas.description: This idea applies the logic of Ecological Niche Modeling from biogeography to the problem of cross-genre emotion classification. In ecology, Species Distribution Models (SDMs) are used to understand and predict a species' presence across different environments by learning the environmental correlates of its known occurrences. Analogously, we can treat emotional expressions as the 'species' and different text genres (news, blogs, fiction) as distinct 'ecosystems' with specific linguistic, stylistic, and contextual 'environmental variables.' The analogous solution involves not directly transplanting a model but first modeling the relationship between emotional states and their textual features within a source genre. This model is then adapted for a new genre by calibrating it to the new genre's distinctive feature set (e.g., narrative perspective, formality, use of dialogue, descriptive density). The adapted research idea is to develop a genre-adaptive emotion classification pipeline that explicitly models genre as a conditioning variable, allowing for more robust and accurate emotion and valence detection when moving beyond the constrained ecosystem of news headlines to the diverse landscapes of blogs, social narratives, and literary fiction.

ideas.title: Micro-Scale Summarization via Signal Compression Techniques
ideas.description: This research adapts solutions from Digital Signal Processing, specifically audio compression for very short speech clips, to the problem of very short text summarization. In DSP, specialized codecs like Speex are designed for micro-scale audio, using psychoacoustic models to preserve only perceptually critical components. Adapted to NLP, we propose building a dedicated 'micro-summarizer' model. This model would be trained with a novel objective to act as a semantic compressor for texts under 50 words (e.g., tweets, post titles, single sentences), identifying and preserving the core entities, their primary relationship, and the author's intent with extreme token efficiency, while discarding predictable syntactic structures and filler words, analogous to how audio codecs discard inaudible frequencies.

ideas.title: Discourse-Aware Semantic Graph Construction for Coherent Text Modeling
ideas.description: This research direction proposes to build a discourse-informed semantic graph where nodes are sentences or clauses, and edges are weighted by a combination of fine-grained semantic similarity and predicted discourse relations (e.g., elaboration, contrast, causation). Instead of treating sentences as independent units linked by simple similarity, the model explicitly learns the likely rhetorical function connecting them, using this to constrain and interpret the semantic similarity measures. This creates a unified representation that can distinguish between texts with similar local semantics but different global coherence structures, enabling more accurate modeling for tasks like summarization, question answering, and long-form text generation.

ideas.title: Integrate interpretable STS into downstream applications
ideas.description: Apply interpretable STS models to real-world tasks like question answering or machine translation evaluation.
ideas.sourceTitle: SemEval-2015 Task 2: Semantic Textual Similarity, English, Spanish and Pilot on Interpretability

ideas.title: Disagreement Decomposition for a Manifold of Plausible Emotional Truths
ideas.description: This research rejects the notion that a single high-agreement label constitutes ground truth for subjective tasks like emotion recognition. Instead, it proposes that annotator disagreement is a rich signal that reveals the inherent multiplicity of valid emotional interpretations. The system computationally decomposes disagreement sources—such as cultural perspective, personal experience, or lexical ambiguity—to model a probability distribution or manifold over the label space for each stimulus. This approach formally captures emotional truth as a set of plausible interpretations, enabling AI systems to understand nuance, context-dependence, and minority perspectives rather than learning a forced, reductive consensus.

ideas.title: Multi-Resource Belief Propagation for Contextualized Emotion Inference
ideas.description: This research synthesizes belief propagation on sentence graphs with a multi-resource semantic layer, integrating resources like SenticNet, NRC Emotion Lexicon, and FrameNet alongside WordNet-Affect. The message-passing algorithm operates not just on syntactic/semantic sentence links, but also on nodes representing potential emotion labels from each resource, propagating and marginalizing belief scores across the graph. This allows the system to infer the most probable emotional state for a text segment by probabilistically combining evidence from all resources, weighting them based on contextual consistency and the strength of relational paths in the graph, leading to more accurate and explainable emotion classification.

ideas.title: Challenge single scalar similarity score
ideas.description: Replace the single similarity score with a multi-dimensional vector representing different semantic aspects.
ideas.sourceTitle: SemEval-2016 Task 1: Semantic Textual Similarity, Monolingual and Cross-Lingual Evaluation

ideas.title: Transfer methods from sentiment and emotion analysis
ideas.description: Model the affective and emotional markers associated with deceptive writing.
ideas.sourceTitle: The Lie Detector: Explorations in the Automatic Recognition of Deceptive Language

ideas.title: Scale interpretable STS dataset creation
ideas.description: Develop methods to efficiently create large-scale datasets for interpretable STS beyond expert annotation.
ideas.sourceTitle: SemEval-2015 Task 2: Semantic Textual Similarity, English, Spanish and Pilot on Interpretability

ideas.title: Goal-Conditioned Micro-Text Abstraction for Contextual Utility
ideas.description: This research proposes a framework for very short text processing that moves beyond simple compression. Instead of generating a shorter version, the system produces a goal-conditioned abstraction, where the summary's form and content are dictated by a specified user intent or contextual application. The model is trained to first analyze the micro-text's discourse function and then synthesize a useful representation tailored to scenarios like information triage, cross-platform repackaging, or database indexing. This ensures the output has consistent utility even when the source text is minimal.

ideas.title: Transfer graph matching algorithms to STS
ideas.description: Model sentences as graphs and use graph matching algorithms to compute similarity and alignment.
ideas.sourceTitle: SemEval-2015 Task 2: Semantic Textual Similarity, English, Spanish and Pilot on Interpretability

ideas.title: Challenge well-formed text requirement
ideas.description: Evaluate STS on highly informal, ungrammatical, or noisy text data.
ideas.sourceTitle: SemEval-2014 Task 10: Multilingual Semantic Textual Similarity

ideas.title: Semantic Restart Random Walks for Query-Focused Summarization
ideas.description: This system performs query-focused summarization using a random walk with restart algorithm where the restart distribution is dynamically generated by a neural relevance model. For a given query and document, the model computes a semantic relevance score for each sentence, which directly determines its probability of being a restart node. This ensures the random walk's stationary distribution, used for sentence ranking, is fundamentally guided by deep semantic alignment with the query's intent, not surface-level keywords. The approach is robust to vocabulary mismatch and can capture complex conceptual relationships between the query and the document content.

ideas.title: Incorporate semantic similarity for sentence linking
ideas.description: Replace simple word overlap with deeper semantic measures to build the sentence connectivity graph.
ideas.sourceTitle: Graph-based Ranking Algorithms for Sentence Extraction, Applied to Text Summarization

ideas.title: Transfer methods from visual affect recognition
ideas.description: Adapt models for recognizing emotion in images to the textual domain.
ideas.sourceTitle: SemEval-2007 Task 14: Affective Text

ideas.title: Scale to very short text snippets
ideas.description: Specialize the method for measuring similarity between very short texts like search queries.
ideas.sourceTitle: Measuring the Semantic Similarity of Texts

ideas.title: Consensus-Driven Micro-Summarization for Subjective Content
ideas.description: This research proposes a method for summarizing very short, subjective texts (like social media posts or single paragraphs) where the key content is an emotional or interpretive stance. Instead of seeking a single 'correct' summary, the system is trained on multi-annotator data to produce a micro-summary alongside a confidence metric derived from the level of annotator agreement. This approach redefines 'ground truth' for the task as a distribution of plausible summaries, where high-agreement elements form the core summary and low-agreement elements are either omitted or flagged as contested interpretations. The result is a summarization model that is both highly compact and transparent about the subjectivity of its output.

ideas.title: Text Expansion via Semantic Mass Conservation
ideas.description: This research adapts the inverse problem solution from fluid dynamics, specifically the use of mass conservation to reconstruct complete flow fields from sparse data, to the domain of text generation. The original idea of using a graph to add explanatory sentences to a short seed text is re-framed: the seed text provides sparse 'measurements' of semantic content within a vast knowledge graph. The analogous 'semantic conservation' principle dictates that the core meaning must be preserved and fully accounted for in the expansion, just as mass is conserved in a fluid system. The solution involves using the graph's relational structure not just to find relevant nodes, but to algorithmically 'interpolate' and generate coherent explanatory sentences along the graph's edges. This ensures the expanded text distributes the seed's semantic essence across a longer narrative while maintaining strict relevance and logical flow, governed by the constraint that all added content must be reachable via conserved semantic pathways from the original seed concepts.

ideas.title: Transfer cross-modal alignment techniques
ideas.description: Use methods from image-text alignment for text-text semantic similarity.
ideas.sourceTitle: SemEval-2014 Task 10: Multilingual Semantic Textual Similarity

ideas.title: Syntactic-Pragmatic Control for Affective Abstractive Summarization
ideas.description: This research direction proposes a novel abstractive summarization framework where the primary constraint is not merely fidelity to factual content, but fidelity to the source's affective meaning as conveyed through syntax and pragmatics. The system first uses an extractive module to rank sentences based on both informational and affective salience, analyzing features like sentence mood, modality, discourse relations, and focus structures. These ranked sentences and their syntactic-pragmatic profiles then serve as the guiding scaffold for a controlled abstractive generator, which fuses and paraphrases content while being explicitly trained to replicate or intentionally alter the identified non-lexical affective features. The result is a summarization model capable of producing concise abstracts that strategically maintain the argumentative tone, level of certainty, or implied viewpoint of the original text, achieving a deeper level of semantic and pragmatic preservation than content-only or lexically-driven approaches.

ideas.title: Scale to multi-modal interpretable similarity
ideas.description: Extend interpretable STS to align and explain similarity between text and images or other modalities.
ideas.sourceTitle: SemEval-2015 Task 2: Semantic Textual Similarity, English, Spanish and Pilot on Interpretability

ideas.title: A Conflict-Aware Probabilistic Fusion Framework for Multi-Resource Emotion Lexicon Integration
ideas.description: This research designs a principled methodology to integrate multiple emotion lexicons, directly addressing the noise and conflict inherent in simple combination. It develops a context-sensitive alignment model that maps disparate emotion categories and intensity scales across resources onto a unified representation. The core of the system is a Bayesian fusion mechanism that dynamically weights resource contributions based on their agreement with contextual word embeddings and annotated data, resolving conflicts probabilistically. This results in a robust, high-coverage emotion lexicon that demonstrably improves classification accuracy over any single resource or a naïve union.

ideas.title: Develop cross-lingual STS for more language pairs
ideas.description: Expand cross-lingual STS evaluation and systems to cover a wider variety of language pairs beyond those studied.
ideas.sourceTitle: SemEval-2016 Task 1: Semantic Textual Similarity, Monolingual and Cross-Lingual Evaluation

ideas.title: Dual: Generate model answers from rubrics
ideas.description: Invert the process to automatically produce example answers from a grading rubric.
ideas.sourceTitle: Text-to-Text Semantic Similarity for Automatic Short Answer Grading

ideas.title: Challenge monolingual STS model assumption
ideas.description: Develop STS models that inherently work across multiple languages without separate training.
ideas.sourceTitle: SemEval-2015 Task 2: Semantic Textual Similarity, English, Spanish and Pilot on Interpretability

ideas.title: Adapting Seismological Signal Analysis for Textual Affect Recognition
ideas.description: This idea draws an analogy from seismology, where methods from visual pattern recognition were successfully adapted to analyze 1D seismic signals. The analogous solution involved reinterpreting seismic waveforms as time-series 'images' and transferring convolutional neural network architectures from computer vision. Adapted to the textual domain, the research would treat text as a 1D affective signal (via embeddings or syntactic graphs) and systematically transfer the core methodological framework of visual affect recognition—including local feature detection, hierarchical representation learning, and spatial attention mechanisms—to develop novel architectures for recognizing emotion in text. The goal is to create text-specific models that capture emotional contours, sentiment transitions, and nuanced affective cues in language, mirroring how seismologists adapted visual techniques to detect patterns in geophysical data.

ideas.title: Modeling Text as a Markov Random Field for Discourse Coherence
ideas.description: This idea challenges the sentence independence assumption by directly modeling inter-sentence dependencies and discourse structure, analogous to how computer vision models pixel dependencies for image segmentation. The analogous solution from statistical physics/computer vision uses Markov or Conditional Random Fields to define an energy function over a graph, combining local node potentials with pairwise smoothness constraints between neighbors. Adapted to NLP, sentences become nodes in a discourse graph, connected by rhetorical relations. The research would involve defining appropriate unary potentials (for sentence content) and pairwise potentials (for discourse coherence, entity continuity, and pragmatic constraints), then developing efficient inference algorithms to find the most probable global text structure or to generate coherent multi-sentence sequences, moving beyond local, sentence-isolated processing.

ideas.title: Multi-document semantic clustering
ideas.description: Use the pairwise text similarity as a kernel for clustering a large set of documents.
ideas.sourceTitle: Measuring the Semantic Similarity of Texts

ideas.title: Use latent semantic analysis concepts
ideas.description: Incorporate the idea of latent topics from LSA into the knowledge-based similarity framework.
ideas.sourceTitle: Measuring the Semantic Similarity of Texts

ideas.title: Model emotion intensity and valence as a multi-task ordinal regression with uncertainty estimation
ideas.description: This idea expands the original regression approach for emotion intensity and valence by drawing an analogy from medical image analysis for disease severity scoring. In that field, predicting ordered severity levels (e.g., stages 0-4) is effectively solved using ordinal regression techniques that respect the label order and provide uncertainty estimates, which are crucial for reliable diagnosis. Adapted to affective computing, we propose modeling emotion intensity and valence as a multi-task ordinal regression problem. The model would predict a probability distribution across an ordered scale for each dimension, rather than a single scalar, using a loss function tailored for ordinal data (like CORAL). A key adaptation is the explicit output of an uncertainty metric for each prediction, reflecting the inherent subjectivity and ambiguity in emotion annotation. This approach provides richer, more interpretable, and practically useful outputs for systems that need to understand not just *what* emotion is present, but *how strongly* and *how confidently* it is detected.

ideas.title: Invert the unsupervised-only constraint
ideas.description: Use the unsupervised system as a pre-annotation tool to bootstrap a supervised model.
ideas.sourceTitle: Text-to-Text Semantic Similarity for Automatic Short Answer Grading

ideas.title: Multi-Stage Discourse-Aware Sentence Linking with Relation-Specialized Filters
ideas.description: This system constructs a sentence connectivity graph using a cascaded architecture to ensure robustness and efficiency. It begins with a fast co-reference and key concept matching stage to prune unlikely connections. A battery of targeted, low-parameter classifiers then operates on these candidates to label explicit discourse relations. For remaining candidate links requiring deeper semantic judgment, a contextualized similarity model is applied, but its input is enriched with the discourse context and the outputs of the previous relation classifiers. This approach prevents over-reliance on a single similarity score, explicitly models different link types, and drastically reduces computational cost compared to an all-pairs deep semantic comparison.

ideas.title: Learning Adaptive Graph Structures for Nuanced Emotion Dynamics in Text
ideas.description: This research develops a method to automatically construct emotion graphs from text, where nodes represent fine-grained or blended emotional states beyond basic categories, and edges represent their co-occurrence or sequential relationships. Instead of using fixed thresholds for linking emotions, the system learns optimal, context-sensitive parameters for edge creation and weighting directly from the data, which could vary across domains like literature, social media, or clinical narratives. This results in a dynamic, adaptive model that captures the unique emotional landscape and transition patterns inherent to a specific text corpus. Consequently, the approach provides a powerful tool for analyzing emotional narratives, mental health discourse, and affective storytelling by revealing the latent structure of complex emotional experiences.

ideas.title: Combine crowdsourced and expert STS annotations
ideas.description: Develop hybrid annotation frameworks leveraging both crowdsourcing for scale and experts for quality control.
ideas.sourceTitle: SemEval-2015 Task 2: Semantic Textual Similarity, English, Spanish and Pilot on Interpretability

ideas.title: Relax reliance on human similarity judgments
ideas.description: Develop STS evaluation metrics not grounded in direct human annotation.
ideas.sourceTitle: SemEval-2014 Task 10: Multilingual Semantic Textual Similarity

ideas.title: Inverse: Find most similar snippet in corpus
ideas.description: Given a snippet and corpus, retrieve the most semantically similar text.
ideas.sourceTitle: SemEval-2014 Task 10: Multilingual Semantic Textual Similarity

ideas.title: Graph-Guided, Grounded Text Expansion with Coherence Verification
ideas.description: This system generates explanatory text expansions by first using a knowledge graph to retrieve candidate facts and relationships relevant to a short seed text. A verification stage then rigorously evaluates these candidates for semantic coherence and factual accuracy against a trusted corpus, filtering out irrelevant or contradictory propositions. The final output is constructed by integrating only the verified, high-confidence expansions, ensuring the result is both informative and consistent. This approach transforms the graph from a sole retrieval tool into a component within a larger pipeline designed for robust, grounded text generation.

ideas.title: Dual: Generate text of specified similarity
ideas.description: Create a system that generates text with a target similarity to an input.
ideas.sourceTitle: SemEval-2014 Task 10: Multilingual Semantic Textual Similarity

ideas.title: Factuality-Constrained Abstractive Fusion with Extractive Verification Fallback
ideas.description: This system first performs extractive sentence ranking to identify salient content. It then generates abstractive fusions or paraphrases of these ranked sentences. Crucially, each abstractive output is passed through a factuality verification layer that checks for consistency with the source document's entities, relations, and core propositions. If the abstractive sentence fails verification or has low confidence, the system defaults to a verified, high-ranked extractive sentence, ensuring the summary remains both fluent and factually grounded.

ideas.title: Scale down to sentence-level grading
ideas.description: Adapt the method for grading single-sentence fill-in-the-blank or definition questions.
ideas.sourceTitle: Text-to-Text Semantic Similarity for Automatic Short Answer Grading

ideas.title: Data-Driven Inference of Rhetorical Edge Weights via Network Performance Objectives
ideas.description: This project moves beyond predefined, intuitive edge weights by developing a method to learn optimal weighting schemes from data. The system treats the rhetorical relation types as features and uses an optimization or supervised learning framework to discover their influence on measurable network properties or task outcomes. The resulting asymmetric weight functions are validated by their ability to improve predictive performance or explain structural phenomena compared to uniform or heuristic baselines. This creates a reproducible, objective foundation for incorporating discourse structure into network science.

ideas.title: Modeling Affective Meaning as an Emergent Property of Multi-Channel Linguistic Interaction
ideas.description: This research rejects the premise that any single linguistic level determines affective meaning. Instead, it proposes a computational framework where affective interpretation emerges from the dynamic interaction of lexical, syntactic, and pragmatic channels. The system processes these features in parallel, using an attention mechanism to model how context modulates their relative contributions—for instance, how pragmatic framing can override default lexical valence, or how syntactic structure can intensify or dampen affective load. This interactive approach provides a more robust and psychologically plausible account of how language conveys emotion.

ideas.title: Assume semantic dissimilarity is primary task
ideas.description: Focus on accurately detecting and scoring semantic divergence, not equivalence.
ideas.sourceTitle: SemEval-2014 Task 10: Multilingual Semantic Textual Similarity

ideas.title: Multi-Scale Graph Analysis for Textual Units Using Spectral Clustering
ideas.description: This idea adapts a solution from computational neuroscience—multi-scale community detection in brain networks—to the problem of ranking textual units. In brain connectomics, spectral clustering and hierarchical modularity optimization are used to find functional modules that persist across different resolutions (e.g., neuron-level vs. region-level). We apply this to text by constructing a graph where nodes are variable-scale textual units (sentences, paragraphs, or extracted concepts), with edges based on semantic similarity or co-reference. Running a multi-resolution clustering algorithm (like a hierarchical version of PageRank or spectral clustering) on this graph identifies stable 'conceptual communities' across scales. The result is not a single ranked list, but a hierarchical ranking where the importance of a paragraph is determined by its centrality within its local conceptual cluster at that scale and its connectivity to clusters at other scales, enabling more nuanced, context-aware summarization and analysis.

ideas.title: Integrate discourse structure into STS models
ideas.description: Future work should incorporate discourse-level information to better assess similarity between longer, multi-sentence texts.
ideas.sourceTitle: SemEval-2016 Task 1: Semantic Textual Similarity, Monolingual and Cross-Lingual Evaluation

ideas.title: Rhetorically-Aware Text Expansion via Asymmetric Discourse Graphs
ideas.description: This research fuses the challenge to uniform edge weighting with the inverse problem of text expansion to develop a novel graph-based text generation framework. The core is a discourse graph where edges are asymmetrically weighted based on rhetorical relations (e.g., Elaboration, Contrast, Justification) between sentences, capturing the direction and strength of these discourse moves. The system then performs targeted text expansion by taking a short seed text, mapping it onto this graph, and using the rhetorical edge weights to identify and generate new sentences that fulfill a specific, user-specified rhetorical function relative to the seed. This moves beyond simple addition of relevant content to enabling controlled, coherent, and rhetorically structured elaboration of a core idea.

ideas.title: Transfer belief propagation algorithms
ideas.description: Use message-passing algorithms on the sentence graph to compute marginal importance.
ideas.sourceTitle: Graph-based Ranking Algorithms for Sentence Extraction, Applied to Text Summarization

ideas.title: Discriminative Periphery Detection for Contrast and Novelty Extraction
ideas.description: This system moves beyond simple graph eccentricity by defining the 'periphery' of a document or corpus through a composite model of information density, discourse structure, and entity prominence. A classifier, trained on annotated data, evaluates periphery candidates to filter out irrelevant outliers and retain sentences containing legitimate contrasting viewpoints, novel claims, or under-represented evidence. The final output is a ranked list of sentences that are both peripheral to the main discourse and semantically meaningful, providing robust novelty detection resistant to noise.

ideas.title: Incorporate more diverse text genres
ideas.description: Test STS systems on a wider variety of text types and domains.
ideas.sourceTitle: SemEval-2014 Task 10: Multilingual Semantic Textual Similarity

ideas.title: Ground Truth as a Statistical Consensus: A Bayesian Model for Emotional Interpretation
ideas.description: This idea challenges the assumption that high-agreement labels are the only valid emotional interpretation by drawing an analogy from astronomy's method of determining celestial orbits. In that field, the true state of a system is inferred probabilistically from multiple, noisy observations. The adapted solution is to model emotional 'ground truth' not as a single high-agreement label, but as a latent construct. A Bayesian model would be developed to synthesize all annotator judgments—both high and low agreement—by estimating individual annotator reliability and bias parameters, informed by a theoretical prior. The output is a probabilistic distribution over possible emotional interpretations, redefining ground truth as a statistical consensus that inherently accounts for and explains the spectrum of human emotional response, thereby validating a broader range of interpretations.

ideas.title: Adapt Phylogenetic Network Construction for Semantic Sentence Graphs
ideas.description: This idea adapts solutions from computational phylogenetics to the problem of building sentence connectivity graphs. In phylogenetics, the analogous problem of determining evolutionary relationships was solved by moving beyond simple trait matching to probabilistic models of sequence evolution (e.g., maximum likelihood models on genetic alignments) that infer deep homology. Adapted to NLP, this involves replacing simple word overlap with a method that aligns sentence representations (e.g., embeddings or dependency parses) under a probabilistic model of semantic change. The connectivity graph is then built by linking sentences based on the inferred likelihood of a direct semantic transformation, capturing deeper relationships like paraphrase, entailment, or logical progression, analogous to how phylogenetic networks link species based on evolutionary pathways.

ideas.title: Robust Multi-Strategy Discourse Modeling with Uncertainty-Weighted Fallback
ideas.description: This system moves beyond simply modeling dependencies to creating a robust, generalizable discourse engine. It learns a flexible inventory of discourse relations and document structures from a vast, genre-diverse dataset. A key innovation is an integrated confidence model that evaluates the reliability of its discourse parse for a given text, allowing it to weight the influence of inter-sentence dependencies dynamically. When discourse signals are weak or contradictory, the system gracefully reduces reliance on them, defaulting to a more independent treatment to maintain baseline performance, thus ensuring gains from modeling structure without suffering catastrophic failures on atypical texts.

ideas.title: From Drafts to Final Copy: A Hybrid Text Generation Model
ideas.description: Drawing an analogy from film editing, where the raw footage (ranked sentences) is assembled into a rough cut but the final film is created through abstractive post-production techniques. The analogous solution involves using the best raw material as a strict guide for narrative and content, but allowing for generative techniques to create seamless, enhanced final output. Adapted to NLP, this research idea challenges the assumption that extractive rankings must constrain the final text form. It proposes a hybrid model where an extractive ranker identifies key content, and a subsequent abstractive module, heavily conditioned on this 'editor's draft,' performs fusion and paraphrasing to produce a coherent summary that improves fluency and cohesion while preserving the core informational blueprint.

ideas.title: Apply machine translation evaluation metrics
ideas.description: Adapt metrics like METEOR or TER, which use alignment, for the interpretable STS chunk alignment task.
ideas.sourceTitle: SemEval-2015 Task 2: Semantic Textual Similarity, English, Spanish and Pilot on Interpretability

ideas.title: Multi-Emotion Summarization for Ultra-Short Texts
ideas.description: This research direction develops models to produce emotion-aware summaries for single sentences, headlines, or social media posts. It rejects the constraint of a single dominant emotion, instead representing the text as a mixture or sequence of emotional tones. The core challenge is to distill these overlapping emotional signals into a concise summary that reflects the text's affective complexity, enabling applications like nuanced content filtering, empathetic chatbot responses, and advanced sentiment analysis for micro-content.

ideas.title: Adaptive Graph Construction for Dynamic Novelty Detection
ideas.description: This research direction develops a framework that jointly learns the optimal parameters for document graph construction (e.g., similarity thresholds, weighting schemes) with the explicit objective of enhancing the identification of novel or eccentric content. Instead of using a fixed graph to compute centrality for novelty detection, the system treats the graph construction itself as a tunable model, optimized to amplify the signal of novelty as defined by low centrality or high eccentricity metrics. This enables the discovery of novel information that might be obscured by suboptimal link choices in a standard graph, adapting the representation to the specific corpus and novelty task. The result is a closed-loop system where the definition of novelty and the data structure built to find it co-evolve for more precise and adaptable information retrieval.

ideas.title: Modeling Headline Emotion as a Mixed-Membership Composition
ideas.description: This idea challenges the assumption that emotion categories in text are mutually exclusive by drawing an analogy from community ecology. In ecology, mixed-membership models solve the analogous problem of species belonging to multiple overlapping communities by representing samples as compositions of several foundational groups. Adapted to headline emotion analysis, this means modeling a headline not as conveying one dominant emotion, but as a probabilistic mixture of basic emotion dimensions. The research would involve developing or applying a mixed-membership model (e.g., an adaptation of LDA) to learn latent emotion profiles from text and represent each headline as a distribution over these profiles, thereby capturing the complex, blended emotional signals often present in media.

ideas.title: Adversarially-Guided Neural Topic Modeling for Emotion-Aware Representation Learning
ideas.description: This redesigned approach employs a neural variational topic model whose latent topic distributions are explicitly regularized to be predictive of emotional context. An adversarial component forces the model to learn topics that capture emotional salience beyond mere thematic co-occurrence. The architecture jointly optimizes for topic coherence and emotion discriminability, producing topics intrinsically linked to affective states. This results in more robust and interpretable representations where topics reflect emotional dimensions like 'joyful socializing' or 'anxious anticipation' rather than neutral themes.

ideas.title: Assume dynamic, context-dependent similarity scales
ideas.description: Model semantic similarity as a non-static score that changes based on user or application context.
ideas.sourceTitle: SemEval-2015 Task 2: Semantic Textual Similarity, English, Spanish and Pilot on Interpretability
