ideas.title: Abstractive Text Expansion via Graph-Guided Sentence Fusion
ideas.description: This research direction combines the inverse problem of text expansion with abstractive techniques to move beyond simple sentence extraction. A graph representation of a source document identifies key concepts and relationships missing from a short seed text. Instead of just inserting these ranked sentences, the system uses them as a basis for abstractive fusion and paraphrasing, generating new explanatory sentences that synthesize information from multiple source nodes. This results in an expanded text that is both informative and coherent, filling knowledge gaps with fluently integrated content rather than disjointed extracts.

ideas.title: Quantifying the Dynamic Interdependence of Headline and Body Text on Emotional Belief Updating
ideas.description: This research challenges the static view of headline emotion by modeling it as an initial affective anchor that is subsequently updated by evidence in the article body. It employs a multi-method, time-series experimental design to track how emotional responses evolve during reading, measuring both implicit (e.g., facial EMG, heart rate) and explicit states. The analysis focuses on the magnitude and direction of emotional belief revision, testing whether incongruent body information reliably overrides headline-driven affect. The outcome is a computational model of affective interdependence, providing a robust framework for understanding media persuasion and misinformation resilience.

ideas.title: Use Dynamic Topic Modeling to Track Emotional Trajectories in Narrative Text
ideas.description: Drawing an analogy from epidemiology's methods for tracking disease outbreaks, this idea adapts the solution of spatio-temporal scan statistics to the domain of NLP and emotion analysis. In epidemiology, tools like SaTScan analyze time-stamped location data to identify significant clusters of disease cases against a background baseline, revealing dynamic patterns of spread. Adapted to the research context, this involves applying Dynamic Topic Modeling (DTM) to a time-ordered corpus of text. The method will infer latent topics and their evolution over discrete time slices, then analyze these shifting topic distributions to identify significant 'outbreaks' or trajectories of specific emotional contexts (e.g., when topics associated with anxiety surge and converge with topics about a specific event). This moves beyond static correlation to model the temporal dynamics of how emotional context emerges and changes in discourse.

ideas.title: Mapping Nuanced Emotional Trajectories in Long-Form Narrative and Personal Writing
ideas.description: This research proposes developing and applying fine-grained, multi-dimensional emotion models to analyze long-form, narrative-rich text genres such as novels, memoirs, and personal blogs. The goal is to track and classify subtle, blended, and transient emotional states (e.g., nostalgic melancholy, anxious anticipation) as they develop and shift across sentences, paragraphs, and chapters. This approach will require new annotation schemes and computational models that capture emotional valence, arousal, and nuance within specific narrative and personal contexts, offering deeper insights into character development, authorial tone, and the emotional arc of extended discourse.

ideas.title: Incremental Emotion-Aware Topic Graphs for Streaming Sentiment Dynamics
ideas.description: This research develops a system that constructs and continuously updates a dynamic graph where nodes represent topics inferred via an online variant of Latent Dirichlet Allocation (LDA) and edges represent co-occurrence or semantic relation. Each topic node is enriched with a real-time emotional context vector, derived from incremental sentiment analysis of the streaming text assigned to that topic. The core algorithmic challenge is to efficiently update the graph structure, topic distributions, and their associated emotional signatures as new data arrives, without full reprocessing. This results in a live, interpretable dashboard that visualizes not just what topics are trending, but how the collective emotional response (e.g., joy, anger, fear) around each topic is changing over time.

ideas.title: Task-Adaptive Graph Construction via Joint Optimization of Structure and Downstream Objective
ideas.description: This research develops a framework that learns to construct graphs whose topology is explicitly optimized for a specific downstream machine learning task, such as node classification or link prediction. Instead of seeking a universal optimal parameter set, the method jointly optimizes the graph construction process (e.g., similarity thresholds, kernel choices) and the parameters of a model performing the task on the resulting graph. This is achieved through a bilevel optimization or meta-learning setup where the inner loop trains the task model on a graph defined by current construction parameters, and the outer loop updates those parameters to maximize final task performance. The outcome is a principled, automated pipeline that produces context-aware graph structures tailored to maximize the utility for the end application.

ideas.title: Multi-Scale Overlapping Topic Modeling with Coherence-Constrained Segmentation for Discourse-Aware Text Structuring
ideas.description: This system first constructs a semantic graph from text units but detects overlapping, multi-resolution communities to capture topic hierarchies and blended content. Instead of a single hard segmentation, it generates a probabilistic topic affinity matrix for each sentence. A final segmentation is then produced by optimizing boundary placement with constraints for local coherence, narrative flow, and user-specified granularity (e.g., few broad sections vs. many detailed subsections). This results in segments that are topically cohesive while respecting the natural discourse structure and transitions present in the text.

ideas.title: Invert Centrality for Novelty Detection via Anomaly Detection in Sensor Networks
ideas.description: Drawing an analogy from anomaly detection in wireless sensor networks, where novel events are identified by finding nodes with low correlation or high residual behavior relative to their local neighborhood, this idea adapts the solution to text analysis. The method inverts centrality for novelty detection by modeling a document as a semantic network. It ranks sentences not by high centrality (common information) but by a computed 'residual centrality'—a measure of their deviation from the local semantic consensus of their nearest neighbors in an embedding space. Sentences with high residual centrality, indicating low correlation to their immediate context, are extracted as novel or contrasting information, effectively challenging the document's core assumptions by highlighting its semantic outliers.

ideas.title: Hierarchical emotion modeling with categorical priors and conditional regression
ideas.description: This approach reframes emotion recognition as a structured prediction problem. A neural network first outputs a probability distribution over discrete emotion categories, acting as a psychological prior. For each emotion with sufficient probability, specialized subnetworks perform intensity regression, while a final valence prediction is generated as a weighted combination of the valences associated with the active emotions, regularized for consistency. This ensures predictions are anchored to recognizable emotional states while capturing continuous intensity nuances, improving robustness and interpretability.

ideas.title: The Pragmatic Field: Modeling Affective Meaning from Syntactic and Discourse Context
ideas.description: This research inverts the focus from lexical features to syntactic or pragmatic features for determining affective meaning, challenging the assumption that lexical semantics alone is sufficient. Drawing an analogy from quantum chemistry's Born-Oppenheimer approximation, which solves for emergent electronic properties by treating atomic nuclei as a fixed field, we adapt this solution to linguistics. The adapted idea treats lexical semantics as a static baseline 'field' and models affective meaning as an emergent property dynamically calculated from the interactions of words within specific syntactic structures and pragmatic discourse contexts, thereby proposing that the true determinant of affective meaning lies in these higher-order linguistic features.

ideas.title: Apply Quantum State Composition to Sentiment Compositionality
ideas.description: This idea adapts the solution to the problem of composing quantum states from Quantum Information Theory to the challenge of sentiment compositionality in NLP. In quantum mechanics, the state of a composite system is computed via the tensor product of subsystem state vectors, following strict formal rules. We propose to analogously model words as having emotional 'state vectors' within a formal affective semantic space. A compositional operator, inspired by the tensor product and subsequent projection operations, would be defined or learned to combine these vectors into a phrase-level emotion representation. This provides a rigorous, mathematically grounded alternative to existing ad-hoc sentiment composition methods, potentially capturing complex, non-linear emotional interactions within phrases.

ideas.title: Headline Emotion as a Context-Dependent Signal Modulated by Article Body
ideas.description: This idea challenges the assumption that a headline's affective content is self-contained by drawing an analogy from cognitive neuroscience, specifically visual perception. In vision, the perceived color of an object is modulated by its surrounding context (e.g., simultaneous contrast), a problem solved by the brain's comparative processing of local features against a global scene. Adapting this solution, the research proposes that headline emotion is a context-dependent signal. The emotional perception of a headline (the local feature) is not fixed but is dynamically resolved and potentially altered by the informational and emotional context of the article body (the global scene). An experimental study would test this by measuring emotional responses to headlines in isolation versus in their native article context, predicting significant contrast or assimilation effects in perceived valence and arousal.

ideas.title: Emotional Arc Segmentation via Narrative Spectroscopy
ideas.description: This research adapts the core methodology of astrophysical spectroscopy to scale emotion tracking from headlines to full news articles. In spectroscopy, a continuous light signal is decomposed into a spectrum to reveal constituent elements and their properties. Analogously, here, the continuous text of a full article is treated as a narrative signal. It is segmented (like diffracting light) into sequential units (e.g., paragraphs or fixed-length passages). Each unit is analyzed to produce an emotional spectrum—a plot of intensity across a predefined set of basic emotions (the 'emotional absorption/emission lines'). By tracking how these emotional line intensities shift and interact across the narrative sequence, the model can map the complete emotional arc, identifying dominant emotional tones, transitions, and complex emotional blends at different stages of the article. This provides a high-resolution, compositional view of emotional dynamics, moving beyond a single headline label to a spectroscopic 'fingerprint' of the article's emotional narrative structure.

ideas.title: Bayesian Optimization of Graph Construction via Hyperparameter Transfer
ideas.description: This research adapts a solution from computational chemistry, where Bayesian optimization is used to automate the tuning of complex force field parameters by matching simulation to reference data. The analogous problem in graph machine learning is setting optimal thresholds for link creation and edge weighting, which is currently manual and heuristic. The adapted idea proposes a framework that treats the graph construction process as a parameterized, black-box function whose output graph feeds into a downstream model. Using Bayesian optimization, the system would automatically search the parameter space (e.g., k-nearest-neighbor 'k', similarity cutoff ε, weighting kernel σ) to maximize a target performance metric (like classification F1-score or modularity) on a held-out validation set, thereby learning task-optimal graph construction parameters directly from data.

ideas.title: Incremental Graph Summarization via Epidemic Protocols
ideas.description: This research adapts solutions from distributed systems—specifically epidemic gossip protocols—to the challenge of real-time streaming summarization. The analogous problem of maintaining consistent global state (e.g., average load, top-k items) in a massive, decentralized network is solved by having nodes periodically exchange and merge state with a few random peers, ensuring scalability and robustness. The adapted idea develops incremental graph update algorithms where each incoming text unit initiates a 'gossip process,' propagating its local relationship graph to other summary elements. These elements merge incoming subgraphs, applying decay and pruning mechanisms to maintain a bounded, salient summary graph. This approach enables scalable, real-time summarization of live text streams by replacing centralized graph processing with a decentralized, epidemic-style update scheme, ensuring low-latency incremental updates and inherent fault tolerance.

papers.title: Putting Pieces Together: Combining FrameNet, VerbNet and WordNet for Robust Semantic Parsing
papers.venue: Conference on Intelligent Text Processing and Computational Linguistics
papers.abstract: This paper describes the authors' work in integrating three different lexical resources: FrameNet, VerbNet, and WordNet, into a unified, richer knowledge-base, to the end of enabling more robust semantic parsing.
